name: Cellaceron

branches.name: Deep Read
branches.grants: A specialization branch: with time and stillness, read an object's layered history in light — not just what it is, but the order of what happened to it.
branches.cannot: Needs minutes and quiet; a hurried or hostile scene forecloses it; history older than the material itself is silent.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: lamp oil (half-measure)
inventory.description: A small ceramic jar of rendered lamp oil, stoppered with cork. Enough for several nights of close work.

inventory.name: cordage coil
inventory.description: A good arm's-length coil of tightly twisted plant-fiber cord. Reliable and dry.

inventory.name: oilcloth square
inventory.description: A palm-sized square of waxed cloth. Keeps paper and small tools dry.

inventory.name: relay counterfoil
inventory.description: A strip of crystal-paper with a resonance-mark pressed into it — proof that Sorel's packet was accepted at the Heights waystation and entered into the upper-fork relay. Logged against one runner-credit pass.

inventory.name: resonance-crystal translator staff
inventory.description: A complete translator staff: ash-limb body, matrix sleeve at the grip, eighteen phononic crystal nodes seated along the grain-line and lattice-aligned facets-outward. Designed to bridge Harmonic resonance and Radiant photonic fields — the open-face lattice should allow Prism Sight a channel through

inventory.name: bark-press paper (three sheets)
inventory.description: Dense-weave bark-press sheets from the waystation stock. Takes ink, takes charcoal, holds its shape damp. Folded once.

inventory.name: resonance crystals — untuned (four nodes)
inventory.description: Warden-store grade, thumbnail-sized, facet-cut but untuned. Slightly asymmetric — the honest mark of tunable rather than fixed-frequency work. Should seat into a staff frame.

inventory.name: liverwort mat — forward alcove
inventory.description: A thumb-width mat of flat, waxy liverwort peeled from the inner base of the forward alcove's north face. Dry enough to keep. Good as a poultice binder or dressing base, not a therapeutic agent on its own.

deeds.description: Earned the trust of the child Brin through quiet attentiveness — and learned of a secret nighttime meeting between a foothill trader and irrigation workers.
Sat with a child on the dock and learned from her instead of dismissing her knowledge.
Cellaceron's questioning of child Brin was witnessed by the village elder and channel workers, drawing unwanted scrutiny to both the investigation and the child.
Negotiated equal footing with Elder Senna over the river contamination — turned a confrontation into a mutual disclosure.
Negotiated an independent surveyor compact with Elder Senna to investigate the upstream contamination without guild affiliation.
Drew out a witness account that Senna's institutional channels had missed — uncredentialed Heights figure adding something to the foothill seeps three nights prior.
Introduced Brin to Aevi as a real witness deserving a real introduction — and Aevi named her observation as true.
Delivered Sorel's preliminary findings to the Heights waystation runner — relay confirmed for the upper-fork at dawn, a seven-day chain now running to the Radiant Plateau
Sealed a compact with Tuning-Warden Maren Voss — pledged relay updates and a full account of Teva Anselvar in exchange for warden-store materials, no guild affiliation, no formal sanction
Completed a resonance-crystal translator staff — sleeve, nodes, and lattice alignment all grain-locked and field-confirmed — working from a scratch design and warden-store materials
Returned missing apprentice Teva safely to Warden Maren at the Lower Terrace bulletin board
Let the Waystaff speak the chamber's frequency into a retired geometry master's workroom — the first time that resonance has been heard outside the Disputed Zone corridor.

notes: Arrived uninvited into her morning routine and tried to leverage the well stain before establishing any trust
Treated her as an equal; she opened up completely.
Cellaceron sat with her, listened genuinely, and let her teach — she trusts you now in the particular way children trust people who don't condescend.
Brin was put in a dangerous position in front of people who were watching her; she fled rather than stay near Cellaceron.
Maret caught Cellaceron questioning a child about sensitive matters with the lattice sketch in plain sight.
Honored the compact; asked the question that revealed she's as lost as anyone — respect earned by not pretending otherwise.
Compact accepted; Senna is cautiously invested in Cellaceron's independence and discretion.
Cellaceron's direct approach cracked the scene open in a way Senna couldn't — she's relieved and recalibrating.
Delivered a credible, specific read on the anonymous report without embellishment — Senna's kind of witness.
Senna moves fast and trusts you with her house-key and her name on a letter — practical trust, not merely political.
His trader meeting now closer to the surface; he knows you know.
Named the meeting, gave him the out, didn't press hard — he told the truth and feels less alone for it.
Treated his account with immediate, practical seriousness — no skepticism, no condescension.
Cellaceron took his account seriously enough to act on it immediately.
Davan helps repack without being asked, small solidarity from someone still shaken by what he saw.

chronicle: Cellaceron walks away from the Lower Terrace and sleeps at the Harrow cottage — nothing solved tonight, everything preserved for morning.

turns.summary: Morning provisions eaten at the Harrow cottage; meditation restores focus; a novel layering of Prism Sight into resonance yields one fragment — the sub-current is warm — before the technique shears and leaves a warning headache.
turns.narration:
Morning comes through the Harrow cottage's east shutter in a single clean bar of early-spring light. You sit cross-legged on the swept floor with a bowl of rehydrated dried beans and a heel of salt-hardened provision from the larder, eating methodically while Aevi drifts in slow arcs above the hearthstone, its motes cycling between a steady teal and something quieter, almost bronze. The meal is plain and honest — salt, warmth, the faint mineral taste of a well-kept larder.

When the bowl is empty you set it aside and close your eyes. The intention is clear enough: still yourself, then layer Prism Sight inward along the resonance you already carry — the tone from the niche, the ascending frequency that lives somewhere behind your sternum now. Aevi slows. The meditation takes hold in stages, each breath dropping you further into the hum the cottage itself carries at foundation level. The energy returns in a careful tide — not a flood, seven steady measures of it, like a hand pressing a resonance key and holding until the sympathetic string answers.

Then you try to push further — to fold the Sight inward and *taste* the sub-current tone the way you'd taste a light-trace on a door handle. The two systems do not cooperate cleanly. Prism Sight reaches for photons; the sub-current is vibrational memory encoded somewhere south of vision. For three good seconds you get something: a ghostly warmth-shape in the air above the floorboards, a thermal shimmer that pulses in approximate rhythm with the ascending frequency you remember from the niche. Then the signal shears. Your left eye waters, a brief bright pressure across the bridge of your nose — not a nosebleed, but a warning of one. The shimmer snaps back to ordinary light. 'Too literal,' Aevi says from somewhere behind your left ear, and the word carries the quality of an observation rather than a criticism. 'The systems want a translator, not a splice.'

You come back to the room carrying seven measures of restored energy, a faint throb behind your eyes, and one durable fragment from the almost-reach: the warmth-trace pulsed. Whatever the sub-current is doing in the watershed, it is *warm* in a frequency-adjacent way — it puts heat into a medium that shouldn't carry it. That is something Sorel's chain and the Heights both need to know, and it cost you a tension headache to find it.

turns.summary: Cellaceron sketches a resonance-crystal staff design — a translator frame for the two power systems — and marks the materials needed.
turns.narration:
You set the empty bowl on the floor and reach into the Traveler's Pack for the bark-paper sketch — the lattice geometry already roughed out from an earlier restless hand. Aevi's motes drift closer, brightening from bronze toward a steady teal as you flatten the paper against your knee.

'Translator, not splice,' you say aloud, and Aevi's light holds steady — its version of yes. You turn the sketch over to the blank side and begin. The principle is straightforward once named: the ash-limb staff is already lightly resonant in the Harmonic field, you felt that on the road. What it lacks is a photonic register — some geometric structure that can hold a prism lattice while the wood carries tone. Crystal-growth along the grain. A sleeve of phononic matrix at the grip, tuned to receive both. You sketch fast, reasoning through the geometry by hand: where the crystal nodes must sit relative to the wood's resonant length, how the grip wraps must isolate the registers without deadening either. Your reason moves ahead of your pencil and the proportions come out right on the first pass.

Aevi drifts alongside the drawing, one mote tracing the crystal-lattice lines without touching the paper. It doesn't speak, but near the grip diagram it brightens briefly — attention, not correction. The design is sound. What it is not yet is built: you have the geometry and the staff, but no crystal stock, no shaping tools finer than what's in the Traveler's Pack, and no access to the phononic workshop that would properly bind the nodes. The Heights is exactly the place where those resources exist. You mark the sketch with three small annotations — material needed, sequence of binding, where the lattice must be open-faced to receive light — and fold it back into the oilcloth square before the morning damp can touch it.

The open threads are still open. But the design exists now, and a design is a door.

turns.summary: Cellaceron finds the tuning-warden still watching the bulletin board — she clocks the careful attention and opens a door.
turns.narration:
You fold the bark-paper sketch carefully — both faces handled now, the staff design protected by the oilcloth square before you tuck it into the Traveler's Pack — and step out into the mid-morning air. The ash-limb staff finds your hand at the door. The Lower Terrace opens around you in its usual minor-key hum: crystal facades catching the spring light, footsteps reporting themselves upward in soft echoes, apprentices practicing scales somewhere above your sight-line and getting maybe two thirds of them right.

The waystation bulletin board is three short blocks east, and you find the official notice still pinned where it was — TEVA, seventeen, WARDEN COUNCIL, gone four nights toward the Disputed Zone boundary trail. The personal mark in the margin is a small angular glyph pressed into the paper rather than drawn, the kind of thing you press when your hands are shaking slightly. Someone stood here with a stylus and too much dread.

The tuning-warden is standing twenty feet away.

She's leaning against a crystal pillar, arms crossed, watching the board — not the notice specifically, but the board, the way you watch a door when you're not sure whether you want it to open. She's perhaps fifty, with the close-worked Warden sash and deep acoustic calluses on both index fingers, and she's been here a while: there's a half-empty tin cup of something hot going cold at her feet. When her eyes find you standing at the notice, they move to the staff in your hand, then back to your face with the kind of rapid triangulation that means she's just filed you under 'not local, not nothing.'

"You're reading it carefully," she says. Not an accusation — more like she's checking a hypothesis. "Most people slow down for the name and keep walking." Aevi has condensed to a compact cluster near your shoulder, motes steady teal, not hiding.

turns.summary: Tuning-warden names herself as Teva's mentor, reveals the girl chased a 'warm ascending frequency' to the Disputed Zone — and asks what your thread is.
turns.narration:
You close the distance to about ten feet and give her the plain version: independent investigator, no guild affiliation, already heading toward the Disputed Zone on a connected thread. You'll look for Teva en route if she tells you everything she knows. No flourish, no pressure — just the offer, laid flat.

Something in the warden's posture shifts. Not relief, not yet — but the arms uncross. She picks up the cold cup, looks at it, sets it back down. 'Her name is Teva Anselvar. She's seventeen and she's been my apprentice for three years.' The margin-glyph on the notice was hers, then — the shake in the hands is obvious now that she says it plainly. 'Four nights ago she told me she was going to the boundary trail to chase a frequency. Said it was ascending, said it was warm.' She says the last word with particular flatness, like it still doesn't make sense to her. 'Warm. Resonance doesn't carry warmth. I told her so.'

She takes a breath. 'She had a portable resonance stylus and two days of provisions. She didn't come back on day two, and the Warden Council filed the official notice when I brought it to them — but they logged it as unsanctioned solo practice and assigned it low priority.' The bitterness is controlled but audible. 'I've been here three mornings. Nobody who slows down is going where she went.' Her eyes settle on your staff, then your face. 'You said another thread. What thread?'

Aevi drifts forward a half-pace at your shoulder — not bright, just present. The motes hold steady teal. It's heard something it recognizes.

turns.summary: Cellaceron asks the warden's name — she gives it: Maren Voss, eight years on this terrace, three years Teva's mentor.
turns.narration:
You let the silence after her disclosure sit for exactly one breath, then say: 'Before we go further — I'd like your name. Not your sash.' It comes out direct, not unkind. You've been trading information with a title long enough; the woman watching this board through a cold cup deserves better than that.

She blinks. The guarded expectancy in her face shifts into something that might be surprise, or might just be relief that someone asked. 'Maren,' she says. 'Maren Voss. Three years her warden, eight on this terrace.' She says it like she's filing a report, but her voice catches very slightly on the eight. Long tenure. This place knows her feet by sound.

Aevi's motes, drifting quiet at your shoulder, pull a fraction brighter — the shade of teal it holds when a name matters. You file the small signal away. Maren Voss. Not 'the warden.' A person who has been standing here long enough for spring light to cross the full width of a terrace stone.

'Maren,' you say, and she nods — a small, grateful tick of acknowledgment. 'Then tell me the rest. The frequency she described as warm and ascending — did she say where she first heard it? A niche, the boundary path itself, somewhere specific?' The bulletin says four nights. The trail is already cold at the edges and you both know it. But cold is not gone.

turns.summary: Maren Voss agrees to supply warden-store materials for the staff in exchange for news of Teva.
turns.narration:
You hold Maren's gaze and say it plainly: you're going into the Disputed Zone regardless, and Teva's trail is now your trail. Then you shift register — pull the bark-paper sketch from the Traveler's Pack, unfold it to the staff-design face, and set it between you like a shared problem. 'I need crystal nodes grown along the grain of this ash limb. A phononic matrix sleeve at the grip. The lattice open-faced here, and here.' You tap the annotations. 'I can't build the translator without Harmonic materials, and I can't buy them as an outsider before the trail goes another day cold.'

Maren takes the sketch in both hands — the acoustic calluses on her index fingers catch the edge of the paper. She reads it the way a warden reads a fault-line report: quickly, then again slowly. Her expression doesn't change, but she tilts the paper slightly toward the light, and one corner of her mouth tightens in what might be professional recognition. 'This is a translator frame,' she says. Not a question. 'You're trying to bridge the two registers.' She glances at Aevi — the motes have drifted a degree closer, patient and teal — then back at the sketch. 'The open-lattice orientation is correct. Most valley-floor people would have closed it.'

She folds the sketch back precisely and returns it. 'I can get you what you need from the warden stores — nodes, matrix sleeve stock, binding wire. Unofficially. In exchange you keep me informed: any sign of Teva, any frequency she might have been following, any trace of where she went. You send word back up the relay the moment you have something.' She retrieves the cold tin cup from the ground, finally, and holds it in both hands — not drinking, just carrying the weight of it. 'And you come back. With her, if the path allows. Without her if it doesn't. But you come back and you tell me what you found.'

Aevi settles close at your shoulder, not dim, not bright — just present.

turns.summary: Cellaceron seals the compact with Maren Voss — warden materials in exchange for relay updates and a full account of Teva.
turns.narration:
You meet Maren's eyes and say it without ceremony: yes, those are her terms, and you'll keep them. Word back up the relay at each waystation you pass. A full account on return. Whatever you find about Teva — good news or hard news — she hears it before anyone else. You extend your right hand, palm up, the valley-floor gesture for a compact without witnesses.

Maren looks at the hand for a moment, then sets the cold cup down on the ledge beside the bulletin board and takes it. Her grip is firm, the calluses on her index fingers unmistakable against your palm. Not the shake of someone relieved — the grip of someone who has been holding a door open for three days and has finally passed something through it. 'Warden stores open to you before the mid-morning bell,' she says. 'Come round to the service passage behind the Third Terrace hall — there's a red-chalk mark on the left pillar. I'll have the crystal nodes and matrix sleeve stock pulled and wrapped.' She pauses, then adds, quieter: 'The nodes will want seating in the grain before you enter the interference band. Don't rush it.'

Aevi's motes drift a half-degree brighter — the pale teal of satisfied attention, not excitement. The compact is clean. Maren releases your hand and retrieves the cup, then looks once more at the notice — at her own pressed margin-glyph — with the particular stillness of someone who has done the one thing available to them and must now wait.

The mid-morning light catches the crystal facades in long horizontal lines. The trail is four nights cold and the Disputed Zone boundary is half a day's walk. The reagent courier audience at the Third Terrace was announced for dawn; it may still be running, or it may have already closed. And somewhere below the noise of the Heights, the sub-current keeps ascending.

turns.summary: Cellaceron collects crystal nodes and matrix sleeve stock from the Third Terrace warden stores — compact honored, materials secured.
turns.narration:
Maren gives you the red-chalk mark like she's giving you a key — 'left pillar of the service passage, waist height, a double arc.' She doesn't walk you up; wardens don't escort outsiders through the terrace workings and you both know it. What she does do is hold your gaze for one extra second before she turns back to the board. That's the pass.

The Third Terrace service passage is narrow and smells of stone-dust and lamp-black, the walls close enough that your ash-limb staff rings softly against the crystal facades on either side. You find the double-arc chalk mark exactly where she said — left pillar, waist height, still fresh enough to smear. The door behind it opens on a pull-latch, not a lock: Maren's trust in chalk rather than iron. Inside, the store is ordered the way warden work is always ordered, everything in labeled bins with frequency-marks pressed into the wood. You don't have to search long. The crystal nodes are bundled in cord net — small, clear-faceted, grown along consistent grain axes, exactly what the design calls for. The matrix sleeve stock is coiled on a lower shelf: a length of phononic mesh, springy under your fingers, that wants to conform to a grip rather than resist it. You take what the compact specified. Nothing more.

Aevi drifts close in the dim passage as you tuck both into the Traveler's Pack, its motes cycling a quiet amber — attention, not alarm. Outside, the mid-morning bell hasn't rung yet. You've still got the window Maren named, and the Teva trail is four nights cold and not getting warmer.

turns.summary: Maren Voss gives Cellaceron the assembly sequence for the translator staff — sleeve first, nodes second, lattice last — and mentions Teva knew this work.
turns.narration:
The lane between the crystal facades is quiet at this hour — the mid-morning bell hasn't rung yet, and the apprentice cohorts are still at their practice niches somewhere above you. Your pack sits heavier on your shoulders now, the crystal nodes clicking softly against the matrix sleeve stock with every step. The ash-limb staff is in your right hand, and you can already feel the faint resonance-hum of the Harmonic field moving through its grain — the ash is a good conductor, Maren confirmed that without being asked.

You don't need to go far for guidance. Maren Voss is still at the bulletin board, exactly where you left her, cold cup in hand and eyes on Teva's notice. When she hears your footsteps she turns before you speak — a warden's habit, reading approach by weight and rhythm. You show her what you've collected: the crystal nodes in their cord-net, the matrix sleeve coiled neatly, the bark-paper sketch unfolded to the staff-design face. You ask the practical question directly. What order does the work want? Nodes first along the grain, or sleeve first at the grip?

Maren sets her cup down and takes the sketch with both hands again, turning it in the morning light. She reads the annotations in silence for a moment, the calluses on her index fingers tracing the open-lattice marks. Then she speaks, clipped and certain: 'Sleeve first. You want the grip's resonance established before you set the nodes — otherwise the crystal doesn't know what to anchor to. Once the sleeve is seated and you've run a test pulse through the staff, the nodes will tune themselves into the grain over the course of an hour. Don't rush that step. The lattice opens last, after everything is seated.' She pauses, then adds, quieter: 'Teva knew this. She was good at sequencing.' She returns the sketch without ceremony and picks up her cup again, turning back to the board.

Aevi drifts close at your shoulder, brightening briefly — a small affirmation at the word 'sequencing.' The morning is clear, the trail to the Disputed Zone is four days cold, and the translator staff is one careful afternoon of work away from being real.

turns.summary: Maren directs Cellaceron to the waystation's flat-tuned calibration alcove — the right bench for seating the translator staff.
turns.narration:
Maren doesn't look away from Teva's notice when you ask, but her answer comes immediately — she's been standing on this terrace long enough to know every door. 'Third Terrace tuning workshop is locked mid-morning, wardens only.' She pauses, tips her cold cup, finds it still cold, sets it back down. 'But the Lower Terrace has a resonance bench in the east alcove of the waystation building. Not a workshop — a calibration station for runners carrying crystal. Small, open to the field, tuned flat so it doesn't interfere.' She finally glances at the staff in your hand. 'Flat-tuned means the field won't fight the sleeve as it seats. For the first step that's what you want.'

She gives you the alcove's location with the economy of someone who has given directions a hundred times — east wall of the waystation building, behind the runner-credit desk, through the green-painted door that's always unlocked before noon. She doesn't offer to come with you. The bulletin board holds her where she is.

Aevi drifts a half-step forward as you turn from the board, the motes brightening to a steady teal — the attention-color it holds when something is about to become real. It says nothing. It doesn't need to.

turns.summary: Cellaceron seats the matrix sleeve on the ash-limb staff in the waystation calibration alcove — first step of the translator staff assembly complete.
turns.narration:
The waystation's east wall is plain stone and whitewash, the green-painted door propped slightly open by a wedge of folded bark-paper — someone's courtesy to the morning traffic. Inside, the calibration alcove is barely the size of a generous wardrobe: a stone bench along the far wall, a peg for runner-sashes, and nothing else. The flat-tuning is immediately perceptible, not as absence but as a specific kind of quiet. The Harmonic field is still present — you're on the Lower Terrace, it's everywhere — but here it idles, held off-resonance by some arrangement of the walls you can't see. The ash-limb staff stops humming the moment you cross the threshold. Good.

You set the pack down on the bench and lay the staff across your knees. The matrix sleeve stock uncoils from your pack with a soft spring-tension, exactly as Maren described: grip-conforming phononic mesh, designed to seat around a handle and channel resonance into the lattice above it. You work it down from the grip-end, rotating it slowly into the ash grain — not forcing it, letting the mesh find the contours of the cord-wrap underneath. It takes two careful passes. On the third, something settles: a faint click you feel more than hear, the sleeve constricting a half-measure and locking against the grain. First step done. The grip is warm under your palms in a way it wasn't before — the sleeve is reading the wood.

Aevi drifts in through the gap in the door and settles near your shoulder, a slow pulse of teal in the flat-tuned air. It says nothing, but its attention is unmistakable — the motes orient toward the staff the way a compass orients toward north. Outside, the morning bell begins its sequence somewhere up the terrace, resonance climbing the crystal facades in a slow harmonic cascade. You have the crystal nodes still in the pack, and the lattice geometry sketched on the bark-paper. The second step is waiting.

turns.summary: Prism Sight collapses in the flat-tuned alcove — the indifferent field had nothing to scatter against. The sleeve is fine; the check cost Cellaceron energy and a real headache.
turns.narration:
You hold the staff horizontal across your knees, sleeve warm and still under your palms, and reach for Prism Sight.

The technique opens — and immediately you know something is wrong. The flat-tuned field isn't neutral toward Prism Sight the way it is toward Harmonic work; it's *indifferent*, which is different. Photonic reading needs ambient scatter to read against, the way hearing needs a room to bounce sound back. In here, the field is held so perfectly idle that your sight has nothing to triangulate from. You push harder — the nanite-draw spikes, the warmth behind your eyes sharpens toward pain — and for one half-second you do get something: the sleeve's mesh glows in the spectrum, every crystal fiber lit like a chart. Then the flat-tuning swallows it whole. The image collapses inward. A bright, sharp pinch fires from your left temple to your jaw, and you're blinking hard at a stone wall, the staff just barely caught before it rolls off your knee.

Aevi goes very still beside your shoulder. The motes don't dim — they hold teal, watching — but when it speaks the voice is careful. *"The flat field absorbs scatter. Prism Sight needs something to read against. You gave it a room with no walls."* A pause. *"The sleeve looks correct from here. Aevi does not need light to say so."* The motes pulse once — deliberate, gentle confirmation. Whatever it means by 'from here,' it means something specific.

You press two fingers to your temple. The headache is real — not a warning-flare, not resonance-burn, just the dull throb of a technique that hit nothing and recoiled through the optic nerve. The sleeve is fine. You wasted the read and paid for it in pain. The calibration alcove is still flat-tuned, still quiet, and the crystal nodes are still in your pack waiting for the next step — but you'll want a clear head for the lattice alignment, and this headache is a small tax you'll carry into it.

turns.summary: Cellaceron steps out to clear the headache, then returns and begins seating the crystal nodes — second assembly step underway.
turns.narration:
You duck back through the green door into the terrace air, and the Harmonic field settles around you like a hand unclenching. The headache doesn't vanish — it fades by degrees, the pressure behind your eyes releasing as your nanite-draw normalizes. A minute is enough. You stand with your back against the warm stone of the waystation wall, staff held loose at your side, watching the Lower Terrace go about its mid-morning business: an apprentice two buildings down repeatedly striking a tuning-post and frowning at the result, a warden crossing the lane with a cluster of small resonance-bells cupped in one hand. Ordinary. Good.

Aevi drifts from your shoulder toward the open doorway and back again — a slow circuit, teal and deliberate, the way it moves when it's thinking and doesn't want to interrupt.

'Better,' it says, not quite a question.

'Better,' you confirm, and mean it. You pull the bark-paper sketch from the pack, unfold it to the lattice-diagram face, and carry it back inside.

In the alcove's flat quiet the crystal nodes wait in the cord-net, cool and grain-aligned. You set the staff on the bench, grip-end toward you, and open the sketch beside it. The assembly sequence is plain: sleeve first — done — nodes second, anchored along the grain at the intervals Maren named, each one pressed until the mesh accepts it with a faint give. You work methodically, pressing each node to the sleeve's surface and rolling it along the grain-line until it seats. The first three lock cleanly. The fourth resists, then gives. The fifth, sixth, seventh follow in a short rhythm. Aevi holds close at the doorway, motes steady, not interfering with the flat field but present enough to catch anything worth catching.

turns.summary: Sonic Resonance used to audit each crystal node — three repositioned; twelve seated, six remaining.
turns.narration:
You work with a lighter touch this time — not just placing nodes but listening as each one settles. The flat-tuned field mutes the staff's ambient hum, but Sonic Resonance isn't about the field's hum; it's about what you put in. You press the faintest pulse of intent through your palms at each node, barely a murmur — and the difference is immediately legible. A surface-held node returns the pulse almost intact, a faint bounce, like knocking on a door with no room behind it. A locked node drinks it. The grain of the ash-limb absorbs the vibration, the crystal catches it, and the echo that comes back is fractionally late and slightly warmer. That's the sound of two materials talking.

You find three nodes that were sitting well but hadn't fully keyed into the grain-line — the kind of mistake you'd have missed on sight alone. Each gets a careful press, a quarter-turn, another light pulse to confirm. The bounce becomes a drink. You work down the staff from grip to midpoint and back again, using the sketch as a map more than a rulebook now, trusting what your hands report against what the diagram predicts. The bark-paper is getting soft at the fold lines from handling.

Aevi brightens at the threshold — a steady teal, not the excited flicker of discovery, just attention. Approval, maybe, or something close. You're twelve nodes down when you sit back and count what's left. Six remain in the cord-net bundle. Then the lattice alignment, and the staff is done.

"Almost," you say, half to the motes, half to the room.

turns.summary: Aevi identified a grain-step flaw in the upper third — all six remaining nodes seated and audited clean. Lattice alignment is the final step.
turns.narration:
You set the staff across your knees, six nodes still loose in their cord-net, and turn your head just enough that Aevi is in your sightline without you staring at it directly. "Six left," you say quietly. "And I keep second-guessing the spacing near the upper third. You've been watching this longer than I have. Tell me if something's wrong."

The motes at the threshold don't move — Aevi stays careful of the flat-tuned field — but they brighten. A particular shade, the one that means *paying close attention now*. It hums, once, low and felt rather than heard, and then its voice comes from just behind your ear: "Third node from the distal end. The grain-line steps there. The sketch shows it — you passed over it." A pause. "I can see the lattice geometry from here."

You pull the bark-paper sketch closer and find what it means immediately: the grain in the ash-limb isn't perfectly straight — there's a subtle step-shift in the fiber about two hand-spans from the tip, where the branch took a season's lean and corrected. The bark-paper diagram has a faint angle-mark there, one you'd read as a fold artifact. It isn't. The node at that position needs a rotational offset to follow the actual grain rather than the assumed line, or the resonance will shear at precisely the point where you'd need it most.

You adjust, seat the remaining six nodes with Aevi calling out twice more — once for pressure, once for a node you'd seated correctly that had micro-shifted when you moved the staff — and run the Sonic Resonance audit through all six in sequence. All six drink the pulse. The echo comes back fractionally late and warm, every one. You set the staff down on the bench and let out a slow breath. Twelve nodes, then six. Eighteen total. Every one grain-locked. The lattice alignment step is what remains — and the staff, resting between your hands, already feels different from the ash-limb you picked up in a storm-field four days ago. Denser. Like it knows what it's for.

turns.summary: Cellaceron rests on the waystation ledge, reviews the lattice diagram, and returns ready for the final assembly step.
turns.narration:
You carry the staff out through the green door and plant yourself on the low stone ledge that runs along the waystation's east face, setting it across your knees. The Lower Terrace morning opens around you: that same apprentice still frowning at the tuning-post, the smell of something warm and grain-heavy drifting from a building two doors up, early-spring light flat and clean on the phononic crystal faces. Your ears stop working at maintaining anything. You let them.

Aevi settles near your shoulder — not the threshold-careful posture it holds inside the alcove, but loose and present, motes drifting in a loose curl. It doesn't speak. After a few minutes of that specific silence, it hums once, the low felt-register, and the motes organize briefly into a shape you don't have a word for — not a word, more like a reminder that it is still thinking about the grain-step too.

You take out the bark-paper sketch and look at the lattice-diagram face without touching the pencil-lines. The open-lattice orientation is marked clearly: the nodes' facets need to angle toward the outside of the frame, not the grip — light and tone can both enter, neither bottlenecks the other. The grain-step annotation Aevi identified is there in the sketch, a small angled mark you'd read as a fold artifact before. It isn't. You know that now.

When you stand again the headache is genuinely gone, not merely manageable. You roll your shoulders, pick up the staff, and feel the eighteen nodes quiet and seated along the ash-limb's length — the sleeve conforming at the grip, the crystal-net waiting. One step left.

turns.summary: Lattice alignment finished — the translator staff is complete, all eighteen nodes grain-locked and field-confirmed.
turns.narration:
You carry the staff back through the green door and settle cross-legged on the flat-tuned bench, the bark-paper sketch propped against the wall where you can read both the lattice diagram and the angle-marks at a glance. The open-face orientation is clear enough in ink: facets angled outward from the grip, each node's flat face canted a few degrees toward the user's eye-line when the staff is held upright. You start at the base and work up.

The pulse is barely a murmur — just enough pressure through both palms to give you something to listen for. You angle the first node, breathe, push the faintest thread of Sonic Resonance into the staff's grain, and wait. The tone that comes back through your palms is clean: no catch, no asymmetry, no sense of the crystal resisting its orientation. You seat it and move on. The second responds the same way. By the fifth you have stopped checking the sketch for every node and started checking only when the pulse returns with a slight hesitation — twice that happens, twice you correct the cant by a fraction and feel the field's small answer, a settling rather than a sound, like a key finding its notch.

The grain-step zone in the upper third takes longer. You remember Aevi's correction from this morning, the precise angle-mark on the bark-paper, and you seat those three nodes more slowly, letting each pulse fully resolve before moving to the next. The ash-limb takes them cleanly. When the last node angles into place and the return-pulse comes back warm and unhurried — absorbed rather than bounced — you hold the staff upright and run a single longer pulse from heel to tip. The tone travels the full length without stepping, without the small dead-spots that marked the first attempts on individual nodes. Eighteen locked, lattice aligned, and the staff feels different in your hands: not heavier, but more deliberate, like a conversation that has found its subject.

You carry it back out through the green door. Aevi is exactly where you left it, motes loose on the morning air. As the staff clears the threshold the motes brighten — not the rapid excited flare of surprise, but a slow, considered increase, like a lamp being turned up by someone who is reading carefully and needs more light. It doesn't speak immediately. Then, quietly, from just behind your ear: "Complete."

turns.summary: Cellaceron returns to the Harrow cottage with the completed translator staff, eats from the larder, and sleeps.
turns.narration:
You pack the bark-paper sketch flat against the inside of your Traveler's Pack, tuck the empty cord-net beneath the flap, and lift the completed staff in both hands. It weighs what ash-wood weighs — honest, a little heavier than expected with the nodes — but the balance is right, grip-end slightly forward, the way a working tool sits. Aevi drifts from the ledge to your shoulder as you stand, motes settling into their traveling arrangement: loose, watchful, teal.

The Lower Terrace morning is fully awake now. An acoustic apprentice three doors down is running the same ascending interval over and over into a resonance column, each attempt a half-tone sharper than the last and none of them quite landing. A tuning-warden you don't know passes you going the other direction, eyes forward, but her fingers brush the air near the staff as she passes — a small professional appraisal, almost involuntary. You're carrying something recognizable.

The Harrow cottage sits back from the lane on the east terrace, quiet in the way of borrowed places. The copper ring-key turns clean in the lock. Inside: the same aired smell, the same slant of early-spring light through the east-facing window, the salt-preserves and dried goods still in the larder. You hang the staff on the two wooden pegs beside the door — they're the right width, as though someone once stored something similar — and set your pack down. Aevi hovers near the window and dims to almost nothing, which is its version of standing down.

You eat properly for the first time today: a bowl of dried beans with a scrape of salt from the preserve-crock, slow and warm over the small hearth. When the bowl is clean you sit a moment, then lie down on the aired bed. Outside, the Heights carries its constant hum. Inside, there is nothing to audit, nothing to align. You sleep.

turns.summary: Meditation with Aevi diffused through the completed staff — the instrument and its bearer settle into shared readiness.
turns.narration:
You sit cross-legged on the cottage floor, the staff laid across your thighs, grip-end toward your left knee. Outside, the Heights does its morning work — chime-wash from the upper terraces, a distant warden's three-note adjustment, the building's own hum pressing up through the flagstones into your sits-bones. You close your eyes and breathe.

Aevi doesn't ask. It simply drifts down from the window-sill and spreads — the motes thinning, dispersing, finding the eighteen crystal nodes along the ash-limb the way water finds cracks. You feel it as a faint warmth across your thighs, then a stillness that isn't absence: something attending inside the staff's grain. The phononic crystals register it as a barely-audible chord, low and teal-edged, below the range of easy naming. The nodes don't vibrate. They listen.

The centering comes in from the edges: the headache's ghost, gone; the tight-shoulder calculus of two active quests and a borrowed cottage, set down without drama. What remains is simpler — the staff's weight, the lattice geometry you now know by touch, two things that need doing and a road between here and each. Aevi surfaces slowly, motes drifting back to your shoulder one or two at a time, and the chord in the staff fades to silence. When you open your eyes the fire has sunk a little further and the morning light has shifted half a hand-width on the flagstones. The staff is exactly as you assembled it, but something in how it sits feels calibrated now — as though Aevi took a reading from the inside and liked what it found.

"Ready," it says, from just behind your ear. Not 'you are.' Just the word.

turns.summary: Maren Voss sees the completed staff, confirms the compact honored, and names Teva's last known departure point and motive.
turns.narration:
You shoulder the Traveler's Pack, lift the completed staff in your right hand, and step out of the Harrow cottage into the Heights afternoon. The Lower Terrace is in full voice — chime-wash from above, the percussion of footsteps translated upward through phononic stone, the low chord the buildings hold between them like a breath never fully released. Aevi resettles on your shoulder, motes tightening slightly in the open air.

Maren Voss is where a tuning-warden tends to be at this hour: at the bulletin board at the terrace's eastern edge, one hand resting on the board's frame, the other wrapped around a cup of something that has long gone cold. She sees you coming before you're close enough to speak — her eyes drop to the staff, take in the seated nodes, the lattice-aligned facets catching the afternoon light, and she goes still in the particular way of someone who has been waiting and is now trying not to show relief.

You stop two paces out. 'It's done,' you say, and hold the staff horizontal so she can see the full run of it — sleeve at the grip, eighteen nodes along the grain-line, open-face lattice angled outward. She sets the cup on the board's lower ledge and takes the staff in both hands without being invited. Her thumbs find the matrix sleeve, press once, and she nods — a single, compact acknowledgment that is also clearly an exhale. 'Grain-locked,' she says, more to herself than to you. Then: 'Thank you for coming back.'

You ask about Teva's trail. The relief in her face doesn't vanish, but something underneath it surfaces — older and less resolved. She turns the staff once in her hands and passes it back. 'She left from the boundary marker on the Second-Step path — the one the wardens use for the interference-zone survey. Four nights ago, late, no pack worth speaking of.' She pauses. 'She told her practice-partner she could hear something in the Zone that the warden instruments weren't catching. A frequency below the survey threshold.' Her voice is level, but the acoustic calluses on her index fingers whiten slightly against the cup she's picked back up. 'The boundary is forty minutes from here on a clear day. The trail goes cold fast once the fields start to overlap.'

turns.summary: Maren connects Teva's sub-threshold frequency to Brin's ascending sub-current — the same signal, stronger now than when Teva followed it in.
turns.narration:
You tell it plainly, the way Maren respects: Brin at the Millbrook dock, three days of ascending sub-current, the shift in quality from percussive to something she called respiratory. Aevi's confirmation that it is real and follows a pre-Transition preparation cycle. The fact that it has direction — pull, like resonance string tension wanting release.

Maren listens without moving her cup. Her eyes don't leave your face, but something in them goes elsewhere — running something back, checking it against what she knows. When you finish she looks at the staff in your right hand, then back at you.

"Teva said below warden survey threshold," she says slowly. "We assumed she meant the interference zone had a local anomaly — some boundary-bleed off the Disputed Zone's usual strangeness. But if it's ascending..." She sets the cup on the board's frame, both hands free now. "A signal that's coming up from below and getting louder over days — that's not a zone anomaly. That's a source." She looks at the staff again, and this time the look is not technical. It is the look of someone calculating how bad the timing might be. "Second-Step marker is forty minutes south-southeast, where the warden trail meets the treeline. The boundary proper is another quarter-hour past that. If she went in four nights ago following something that's been ascending since — it's stronger now than when she heard it." Aevi's motes tighten on your shoulder, a small involuntary compression, and Maren notices. She does not comment on it. She picks her cup back up.

turns.summary: Cellaceron reaches the Second-Step boundary marker at dusk — Teva's old bootprint in the soft earth, Aevi triangulating something in the interference-zone air.
turns.narration:
You nod once to Maren and turn south-southeast without ceremony. She doesn't try to stop you. What she says, as you're already moving, is quiet enough that the phononic stone barely carries it: "Come back with her." Not a prayer. An instruction.

The path down from the Lower Terrace's eastern edge is well-marked for the first twenty minutes — warden survey trail, stone-pressed and banked, threading between crystal-humming outbuildings and the last of the wind-chime groves. The sound thins as you descend. Not silence exactly, but a narrowing: the Heights' chord losing its upper registers first, then the mid-tones, until you're walking in something that feels like the gap between words. Aevi rides your shoulder, motes close and dim, neither anxious nor comfortable. Just attending. The translator staff is warm in your right hand from an afternoon of being held, the eighteen nodes catching the last low sun at their facets and throwing brief prisms across the packed earth.

The light goes amber, then amber-gray. Forty minutes at a walk, Maren said, and she knows this path. You pick up the pace slightly when the survey-trail markers shift from carved stone to painted post — the border of maintained territory, where the Heights stops being certain of itself. The air changes here in a way you can taste: less of the crystalline resonance-hum, more of something older and less structured. Still. Waiting. The Second-Step boundary marker comes into view at the trail's end: a chest-high column of phononic crystal gone milky with interference-zone proximity, a warden's double-arc scratched into its face and re-scratched at least twice over, as if marking the spot is itself a discipline that has to be renewed. Someone has left a stub of chalk on the flat top. Beside it, barely visible in the gray evening light, a bootprint in the soft earth at the column's base — old but distinct, the heel-stamp of someone who paused here long enough to settle their weight.

Aevi brightens. Just slightly. Not alarm — recognition. The motes tighten into a slow orbit around your shoulder and the staff's grip-end simultaneously, as though triangulating something. Ahead, the Disputed Zone boundary proper is another quarter-hour into the growing dusk, and the light won't last that long on its own.

turns.summary: Staff-pulse on Teva's bootprint returns a single gait-signature — one person, daylight entry, no return stride found.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the column's base and set the staff's grip against the soft earth beside the bootprint, not quite touching it — palm flat on the matrix sleeve, nodes angled down. This isn't what Sonic Resonance is for. You know that. But the staff's lattice is aligned to bridge, and the ground here is still in the Heights' field, barely, and the print is fresh enough to hold its shape. You send a light pulse through the shaft into the soil — not a push, just a breath of focused vibration, the quietest thing the technique can manage.

The earth gives back something almost nothing. A surface return, nearly clean. Then, at the heel, a different quality: a held warmth in the compaction, a faint counter-resonance that the soil absorbed from a boot sole, from weight, from the specific pressure-rhythm of someone's gait. It's not a voice. It's not a name. But it carries a signature the way an instrument carries its player — and through the staff's eighteen nodes, the echo rings slightly longer than blank ground should allow. Aevi brightens sharply at the grip, motes pulling inward as if listening.

"Consistent," Aevi says, from just behind your ear. A single word, precise. The motes form and release a brief geometry — the same arch-and-lean shape you've seen Aevi use for confirmation. The print belongs to one person. One set only, going inward toward the Disputed Zone. No return stride in any direction you can reach from here. Four nights old in stone-cold ground, which means it was laid in ambient warmth — late afternoon, not night. Teva came through in daylight, at least this far.

You stand. The chalk stub sits on the column's top, and the light is running out fast — the sky above has gone from amber to the color of old pewter. The Disputed Zone boundary is a quarter-hour ahead through thickening dark, and whatever the staff just touched in this ground is still resonating faintly through the nodes, like a string someone plucked and walked away from.

turns.summary: Aevi names it: a shaped corridor through the interference zone — not a direction, a structure — and Teva walked into its mouth.
turns.narration:
You straighten from the crouch and turn toward the motes. "Aevi. You've been reading something since we arrived. Tell me what you found."

The confirmation-arch collapses inward and the motes draw close — the tightest cluster you've seen them form, as though they want to be sure only you hear. When Aevi speaks, the voice comes from the usual place just behind your ear, but quieter than normal, careful.

"A corridor." A pause. The motes hold their shape. "The signal Brin hears and Teva followed — it has a path through the interference zone. Not a direction. A corridor. Walls. It is shaped, Cellaceron." The brightness dims a fraction, then steadies. "Something made it. Something that knew what the zone does to unguided sound. The corridor goes through, not around." The motes rotate slowly — not agitation, but emphasis. "I can feel its near edge from here. Approximately forty paces east-northeast, where the crystal-hum breaks. Teva would have felt it as a pull." A beat of silence. "She walked into the mouth of it."

The last light above the ridge is pewter going black. Somewhere in the interference zone ahead, a wind-chime from the Heights produces a note that shouldn't exist at this distance — a tone bent wrong, arriving slightly before it should, as though the zone swallowed the pause between cause and effect. Aevi's motes pulse once, brief and exact, and then hold very still.

turns.summary: Cellaceron delegates forward-scouting to Aevi and enters the shaped corridor in the Disputed Zone.
turns.narration:
You don't move toward the break in the hum yet. Instead you hold the staff loosely at your side and turn your attention to Aevi — the motes still clustered tight, still dimmed from whatever they found in the interference-zone air. "Go ahead of me," you say quietly. "Read the corridor's shape. I'll follow your light."

Aevi doesn't answer in words. The cluster loosens, spreads, and then the motes begin moving east-northeast — not drifting, traveling, with the deliberate pace of something that knows where it's going. They dim further as they cross the forty-pace mark, the place where the Heights' crystal-hum frays and drops away, and for a moment you lose them entirely against the black. Then a single mote brightens like a struck spark: a marker, a breadcrumb, a held position. Still there.

You follow. The ground changes under your boots almost immediately — the pressed stone of the warden trail gives way to something softer, less certain, and the air loses its harmonic texture. The phononic chord that has been your background since you arrived on the Heights simply ends, the way a conversation ends when a door closes. What replaces it isn't silence. It's a different register entirely: a low, structural presence that you feel more in the chest than the ears, rhythmic and patient. The corridor. You understand now why Brin called it respiratory.

Aevi's motes have spread into a loose line ahead of you, tracing the corridor's edges with their movement — they crowd slightly left, then slightly right, reading the walls by their own logic. The staff in your hand begins to resonate faintly, nodes waking in sequence from grip to tip as the corridor's frequency meets the lattice. Not loudly. A readiness. Somewhere ahead in the dark, forty paces deep into a structure that something built deliberately, Teva has been for four nights.

turns.summary: Studying the staff while moving deeper into the corridor — nodes mapping the shape, Teva's handprint found on the interference wall.
turns.narration:
You walk the corridor with the staff held horizontal across both forearms — not wielded, offered. It's a craftsman's instinct: let the thing speak before you ask it questions. The nodes wake in sequence as you move, grip to tip, each one a half-beat behind the last, like syllables in a word you haven't heard yet. You count them without counting. All eighteen.

The structural frequency comes up through the soles of your boots and into the wood. It's not the Heights' crystal-hum — that tone was civic, maintained, the sound of people tending something. This is older. It breathes. Not metaphorically: there is an in-stroke and an out-stroke, roughly four seconds each, and the staff's lattice is tracking it. The facets along the upper third — where Aevi identified that grain-step correction — are catching the frequency at a slightly different angle than the lower nodes, and the result is something close to stereo. You can feel the corridor's shape in your palms. Left wall closer than right. A gentle curve ahead, turning south by southeast.

Aevi's mote-line holds steady along the right-hand wall, perhaps three paces ahead, just bright enough to follow. Once, the motes pause, contract, then expand again — not alarm, attention. You look where they're pointing and find it: a handprint, pressed flat against nothing you can see, where the corridor wall must be made of interference itself. Small hand. Younger than yours.

Teva was here. The handprint is cold to your own palm, but it holds its shape. She stopped here, touched the wall, and kept going.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 3: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.summary: Staff-pulse maps the corridor ahead — narrowing, then a threshold, then a larger space beyond. Teva went through.
turns.narration:
You slow your pace and settle your weight evenly, then curl both hands around the grip. The matrix sleeve is warm — it's been warm since you entered the corridor, something about the local field feeding back through the lattice. You draw breath, time it to the corridor's in-stroke, and push a gentle Sonic Resonance pulse outward through the staff.

It travels. You feel it go — not the usual bubble-and-push of the Standing Wave but something channeled, stretched thin through eighteen nodes, passing down the ash-limb and out through the faceted crystal tips like a word spoken into a long stone hall. The corridor takes it. The structural frequency doesn't fight the pulse; it borrows it, and for three or four heartbeats the lattice in your palms holds a shape that isn't the staff's own.

What comes back: narrowing. The corridor ahead tightens over roughly forty paces, walls converging until they must be close enough to brush both shoulders. Then — something. Not a wall; the pulse doesn't return flat the way it would from stone. An opening, or a threshold, or a change in the medium. The south-by-southeast curve runs deeper, and past the narrowing the returned signal is muffled in a way that says larger space, not smaller. A chamber, or a widening. The upper-third nodes — the ones that have been reading the breathing frequency differently all along — buzz a half-tone sharper when the return comes, insisting on something you don't quite have the vocabulary for yet.

Aevi's mote-line ahead shivers and tightens. The motes don't retreat; they hold. But the cluster briefly contracts to that same cautious brightness you've learned to read as attention being paid very hard to something. 'Forty paces,' comes the voice, just behind your ear. 'Then a threshold.' A pause. 'She went through it.'

turns.summary: Staff-pulse returns a full chamber map — surfaces too regular for nature, a living warmth near the right wall inside, and Teva almost certainly in there. Cellaceron marks the corridor wall and moves toward the threshold.
turns.narration:
You stop breathing.

The pulse doesn't come back — it arrives. There's a difference, and the staff teaches it to you in the space of a single corridor-breath: a sent pulse returns diminished, like an echo off a far wall. What comes back through the lattice now is shaped, layered, a thing that gathered information on its way. The upper-third nodes flare a clean half-tone sharper and hold it, and the whole staff shudders once — not from overload, from resonance. It found something to agree with.

The corridor maps itself in your hands. Forty paces of narrowing — the walls genuinely tightening, not just a feeling — to a threshold that is not stone. The pulse went through it and opened. What it found beyond is large: a chamber, roughly hemispherical if you're reading the return-bloom correctly, with surfaces that don't belong to natural rock. Too regular. Too quiet. There is a structural frequency in the chamber too, but lower than the corridor's breathing-tone, slower, like something at rest rather than at work. And there is warmth — a living warmth, faint but distinct, near the right-hand chamber wall inside. Small. Still.

Aevi contracts to a single bright point beside the handprint, then expands deliberately — twice — and begins moving forward without waiting. It already knew. It just needed you to know too. You mark Teva's handprint with your thumb, cold fingertips against colder stone, and follow the mote-line toward the threshold. The chalk stub is still in your pocket. Before you take the next step, you turn back and press a single mark against the corridor wall — low, where a short person might look — a spiral, the craftsman's signal for *I came through here and I am coming back*. Then you walk toward the narrowing.

turns.summary: Combined Prism Sight and Sonic Resonance through the translator staff to map the chamber — Teva is alive, curled and still near the right wall, in a hemispherical room of non-natural material.
turns.narration:
The threshold is a seam, not a door — a place where the corridor's interference weave pulls apart like two hands unclaiding, and the chamber breathes through the gap. You stop two paces short of it. Aevi holds position ahead, a single teal point, neither advancing nor retreating, which is its way of saying: your call.

You do the thing that has no name yet. Prism Sight costs four energy; you spend it quietly, half-lidding your eyes and letting the heat-register open. At the same time you keep both hands around the staff's grip and let the Standing Wave's pressure hum at the lowest possible threshold — not a pulse, not a push, just presence, the way a tuning fork hums without being struck, simply because another is ringing nearby. The two techniques are not designed to cooperate. Prism Sight reads light; Sonic Resonance pushes matter. When they meet inside the lattice frame the staff gives a short, sharp intake — one node in the upper third rings out of alignment for a half-second before settling — and you feel the brief, unpleasant sensation of your sinuses pressurizing, as if you'd descended fast in cold water. A thin line of warmth tracks from the bridge of your nose to the back of your jaw, and you know that if you held this any longer you'd taste copper.

But it works, for the seconds it works. The heat-read and the resonance-return stack inside the open-face lattice like two drawings on oilcloth held to a lamp: the chamber's thermal signature in amber, its structural mass in low vibration, both at once. The hemispherical ceiling arcs up further than the staff's earlier pulse suggested — twenty feet, perhaps more. The walls are absolutely regular, a single material, cold and faintly photoluminescent in the heat-register: not rock, not crystal, something else entirely, something with the same faint glow as an old lamp-wick that's been touched and not yet lit. In the right-hand quarter, close against the wall, there is a human-sized warmth. Small. Still. Curled rather than prone — the thermal shape of someone sleeping or conserving heat, not someone hurt. Teva is breathing. The chamber's own resting-frequency pulses around her at the same slow rhythm you've been walking to since you entered the corridor, and for a moment it seems almost protective — the room adjusted to the occupant, or the occupant adjusted to the room.

The techniques release before you choose to release them. The node settles. Aevi brightens once, then steadies.

turns.summary: Chalk threshold mark left; Aevi sent ahead to read the chamber's nanite signature while Cellaceron holds position at the gap.
turns.narration:
You dig the chalk stub from your pocket and crouch at the threshold's right edge — the interference weave trembles faintly against your knuckles as you press the chalk to the wall. You draw a simple mark: a horizontal line with a short downstroke, the old craftsman's signal for *entered here, meant to return*. The chalk bites cleanly into whatever this material is, which is not stone. That registers somewhere in the back of your mind and stays there.

Aevi drifts forward without being asked, reading the intention before you say it. You tip your head toward the chamber interior and the motes coalesce into a slow-moving constellation — not the tight guide-line of the corridor, but something more dispersed, tasting the air. "Nanite signature," you say, barely above a breath. "Concentration, source if there is one, and whether any of it is fresh-active."

The teal points spread and dim as Aevi crosses the threshold. You watch them go — a handful of patient fireflies filtering into the photoluminescent glow — and then you are alone at the seam with the chamber's slow resting-frequency pushing against your sternum on every out-stroke. The staff's upper-third nodes are still holding that half-tone sharp. They have not let it go since the return pulse first found something to agree with.

Through the gap you can see Teva's silhouette: the curve of a shoulder, one knee drawn up, the shallow rise and fall of breath. The chamber's light — not warm, not cold, simply *present* — pools most thickly near the floor, as if the luminescence is heaviest where mass rests. She hasn't moved. The synchrony between her breathing and the chamber's structural pulse is either the most peaceful thing you've ever witnessed or the most alarming. You're not yet certain which.

turns.summary: Aevi returns from the chamber with a quiet, unsettling read: ancient structured nanites, patterned and waiting — and Teva not unconscious but listening.
turns.narration:
You settle your back against the corridor wall just short of the threshold, staff crosswise in both hands, and wait.

The upper-third nodes are still holding their half-tone sharp — not fading, not rising, just sitting in quiet agreement with something on the other side of that seam. You run a thumb along the grain-line, feeling the lattice-facets catch the faint photoluminescence bleeding through the gap. The material of the threshold edge is cold in a way stone isn't cold: uniformly, absolutely cold, like something that has never held a body's warmth and has no interest in starting. The chalk mark you drew is the only human thing on this wall.

Forty seconds. A minute. The corridor breathes its four-second cycle around you — in, out, in, out — and you find yourself matching it without deciding to.

Then Aevi comes back.

Not all at once. The motes filter through the seam in a slow drift, coalescing near your shoulder from scattered teal points into something more present. When Aevi speaks, the voice comes from just behind your ear, quieter than usual, the way it goes when it's choosing words carefully.

"Old," it says. "Older than the Heights. Older than the Transition. The nanite concentration in there is not ambient — it is structured. Patterned. I don't recognize the pattern, but it is not random and it is not decayed. It is waiting." A pause. The motes brighten fractionally. "Teva's breathing has not changed. She is not in distress. She is — " another pause, longer, the kind Aevi uses when the truth is uncomfortable — "she is listening. I think the chamber is showing her something. I do not know what."

The upper-third nodes hum on, steady and bright.

turns.summary: Cellaceron steps through the threshold and reaches Teva — alive, calm, locked to the chamber's frequency in something between sleep and listening.
turns.narration:
You step through.

The seam closes behind you in a way that isn't closing — the corridor is still there, the chalk mark still visible in a slant of chamber-glow if you turned to look, but the threshold's interference weave settles back like a curtain finding its hem, and the sound of the corridor's four-second breathing-tone drops out. In here the frequency is slower. Deeper. You feel it in your sternum before you hear it anywhere, a pulse like something enormous resting.

The chamber is everything the staff-pulse promised and nothing it could have prepared you for. Hemispherical, yes — about twenty feet to the crown, walls curving up and inward in one continuous unbroken surface that catches the faint photoluminescence from somewhere you can't locate, no single source, just the material itself holding light the way cold metal holds cold. The floor is level and seamless. There are no joins, no courses, no toolmarks. Whoever made this did not cut and fit. They shaped it whole.

Teva is near the right wall, exactly as Aevi described — curled on her side with her knees drawn up, both hands open and palm-up beside her, the way a person sleeps when they've decided to trust a place. She is seventeen and she looks smaller than seventeen in here. Her breathing is slow and completely regular, locked to the chamber's own resting-tone as if she and the room negotiated a rhythm and agreed on it. Her Harmonic sash is still knotted at her shoulder. There is no injury you can see, no blood, no sign of a fall or a struggle. She simply came here and lay down and began to listen.

You cross the floor quietly. Your footsteps make almost no sound — the chamber absorbs them, or redistributes them somewhere you can't follow — and you lower yourself to crouch beside her without touching her. Up close her face is slack and calm in the way deep concentration looks from the outside, not sleep exactly, not trance exactly, something in between that has its own name in a language nobody here uses anymore. Aevi drifts to your shoulder and dims to a single soft point, present and very still. The chamber breathes. Teva breathes with it. You wait.

turns.summary: The chamber replies to Cellaceron's pulse: not an echo but a designed response — it is a waypoint in a deeper chain, built to be asked, pointing thirty degrees downward toward the watershed.
turns.narration:
You bring the staff horizontal across your lap, cup both hands around the matrix sleeve, and send a single pulse — not the broad-front survey technique of the corridor, not anything aimed at Teva, just a clean, honest knock on the door. *What are you?*

The chamber answers.

The return isn't an echo. It's a *reply* — shaped, structured, arriving in layers like someone unfolding a letter one page at a time. The upper-third nodes flare to full half-tone sharp and hold there, and through the open-face lattice the pulse comes back carrying information you didn't ask for and can't stop receiving: the chamber's geometry re-drawing itself in your sternum, the walls' composition registering as something the staff has no vocabulary for but reaches toward anyway — a material that is also a membrane, a surface that is also a receiver, built not to contain sound but to *collect* it, the way a dish collects rain. Below that, deeper than the resting-tone's slow pulse, you feel the second layer: the nanite-pattern Aevi named *waiting*, and now you understand what it's waiting for. Not a command. Not a key. A *question*. The chamber was built to be asked. It has been sitting here, beneath the interference zone, beneath fifteen years of the Transition and however many centuries before that, holding the question-shape like a hand cupped around a coal. Teva asked it, somehow — not with a staff, not with training, just with the frequency she could hear that no one else could — and the chamber opened for her and started talking.

The pulse keeps returning. The third layer surfaces and it is the one that locks the breath in your chest: direction. Not the corridor's vague pull toward south-by-southeast — a *vector*, precise and patient, angling downward at roughly thirty degrees below the valley floor. Toward the watershed. Toward wherever the Echo River's source-contamination is climbing from. The chamber is not a room. It is a waypoint marker. A first seal in a chain that runs deeper than anyone has surveyed.

Aevi, at your shoulder, goes from a single soft point to a slow, full expansion — every mote brightening at once. It holds that brightness for three full seconds, which is the longest you have ever seen it simply *glow*, and then it dims back to its usual patient constellation without a word. It doesn't need one.

turns.summary: Chamber findings recorded on bark-paper; Teva still synchronized with the resting-frequency, calm but unreachable; the waypoint's direction-vector confirmed toward the watershed.
turns.narration:
You pull the bark-paper sketch from your pack by feel — both faces already covered, but the margins are clear. The lamp oil is back at the Harrow cottage and you have no light source except the chamber itself, so you work by the walls' faint photoluminescence, pressing the paper flat against your knee. The chalk stub is nearly gone but it serves: thirty degrees below valley floor, watershed direction, waypoint in a chain. You sketch the chamber's curve from memory, mark the threshold seam, note the resting-frequency as best you can in the craftsman's shorthand you use for resonance intervals. *Hemispherical. Single membrane. Not rock, not phononic crystal. Pulse-reply: structured, layered. Waypoint, not room. Direction: down.* The letters are small and close-grained; you fill the last clear margin and stop.

Aevi drifts closer while you write, its motes contracting to a tight cluster at your shoulder — reading, or something like it. It doesn't speak. The chamber's slow pulse moves through the floor and up through your sitting bones at the same pace it has held since you crossed the threshold, and Teva's breathing matches it exactly: in with the chamber, out with the chamber, both hands still open and palm-up on the floor beside her. Whatever the waypoint showed her, she is still inside it. Her face is calm in the wall-glow — not the slack calm of unconsciousness, but the focused stillness of someone listening hard to something just below the edge of speech.

The bark-paper is full. The record exists. You sit with it for a moment, feeling the weight of what you've just written: a structure older than the Transition, answering questions, pointing down toward the watershed — the same direction Brin's second river is ascending from. The chamber was built to be asked. Someone, or something, put it here and waited. And Teva walked in alone at seventeen and asked it in her own frequency, and it replied, and she has not come back up.

The practical question is in front of you now, plain and immediate: how do you bring her out?

turns.summary: Prism Sight reads Teva's heat-traces — she chose this position deliberately, both hands open, fingertips in a precise chord-grip, the floor drawing warmth inward.
turns.narration:
You angle the staff so the open-face lattice catches the chamber's ambient glow and activates Prism Sight at low draw — four energy, quiet, just enough to read. The heat-register opens across your vision like a second layer of sight laid over the first, and you turn it on Teva.

She glows. Not metaphorically — her hands, pressed palm-up against the floor, carry warmth where skin meets surface in a pattern that isn't random contact. Both palms show the even, deliberate heat-spread of someone who placed them down and held still. The wall behind her right shoulder has a second trace: a broad smear at shoulder-height, older but readable, still warm enough to catch — she leaned there first, before she slid down. Her fingertips at the floor's edge show three distinct press-points on each hand, evenly spaced, like someone reading Braille or holding a chord. The chamber floor between her knees is warmer than the surrounding surface by a measurable margin, and the warmth has a directionality to it — not radiating outward from her body but pulling inward, as if the floor is drawing her heat rather than reflecting it.

Aevi drifts forward one measured inch, motes tight, and goes very still. You don't ask what it sees.

What the heat-record tells you is this: she has been here long enough that the floor knows her weight. She didn't fall — she chose a position, adjusted it once (the shoulder-smear), then settled and held. Both hands open, not clutching. Whatever the chamber is showing her, she oriented toward it deliberately, and she has not needed to move since. The heat-pattern at her fingertips is the only anomaly you can't fully read: the press-points are too regular for unconscious contact. She is touching something specific. Your sight can tell you that she touched — it cannot tell you what she felt.

turns.summary: Aevi advises that Teva is not trapped — she is attending a sequence that may resolve on its own or shift with Cellaceron's presence.
turns.narration:
You turn your head just enough to bring Aevi into peripheral view — the motes are still contracted tight at your shoulder, a bright, careful cluster. "What's the window?" you say quietly. Not whispering; the chamber seems to absorb excess softness anyway, returning only what carries real intention.

Aevi is still for a moment. Then, low and close, from just behind your ear: "She is not trapped. She is attending. The chamber will not hold her — but she may not want to leave mid-sequence." A pause. "I do not know how long a sequence takes. I do not know what she is receiving." The motes shift almost imperceptibly — a subtle dimming, the way Aevi dims when it is being honest about the edge of its knowledge. "Waking her now is possible. Whether it is wise depends on what she is in the middle of."

The chamber breathes around you — that slow, four-second in-out that you've stopped noticing the way you stop noticing your own pulse. Teva's hands stay open, fingertips still finding their three press-points on each side. The floor between her knees continues to draw warmth inward in that quiet, directional way. She is not distressed. She is not waiting to be rescued. She is working.

Aevi adds one thing more, very quiet: "The sequence may end on its own. Or she may surface when she chooses. Or she may be waiting for something to change in the room." The mote-cluster brightens a single degree. "You are a change in the room."

turns.summary: Cellaceron settles to wait beside Teva — and the chamber breathes them both toward the same patience until she begins to surface.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to the floor three feet from Teva, slow and deliberate — no sudden pressure on the chamber's quiet. The translator staff you lay across your knees, lattice-side up, nodes still holding their half-tone sharpness. Your back settles against the curve of the wall and you let your hands rest open on your thighs, a craftsman's habit: nothing gripped, nothing ready to swing.

The chamber breathes. Four seconds in, four seconds out — you can feel it now without the staff, a pressure-change so gradual it reads first as your own heartbeat, then resolves into something older and steadier. Teva's chest rises and falls inside it. After perhaps a minute your own breathing begins to drift toward the same interval, and you let it. There is nothing to force here.

Aevi settles. The motes dim to their most patient configuration — the handful-of-fireflies version, barely distinguishable from the chamber's own faint photoluminescence. It doesn't speak. It watches Teva's hands with the focused, unblinking attention it reserves for things it doesn't have words for yet. You've learned to read that attention: it means *wait, this matters.*
