He marks something in the margin, then sets the stick down and looks at the Waystaff properly for the first time since you placed it on the workbench edge. 'Hold it level. Parallel to the grain, not the floor.'

You shift your grip and bring the staff horizontal. Aevi drifts one degree forward — not novelty, not cataloging, the attentive-witness register it uses when something technical is resolving correctly. Farren rounds the bench and runs two fingers along the node line without touching the lattice faces, reading the spacing like braille. 'There.' His finger stops at the third node from the grip. 'That one is carrying residual chamber-tone — you can feel the coolness offset if you run a thumb across the grain at an angle.' You do. He's right: a band of almost-cold in the wood, not temperature exactly, more like a difference in how the surface wants to be touched. 'Don't clear it. That's your reference anchor. Any time you discharge into an unfamiliar space, that node's interval tells the field what register to seek.' He steps back and picks up the marking-stick again. 'The valve is operable as-is. You just needed to know what you were holding.'

Maren, still at the cracked door, has been listening without turning. She speaks toward the morning light rather than the room. 'The upper-fork runner goes at midmorning. Priority Council traffic or not, relay doesn't hold packets once the counter-bell rings.' A beat. 'Farren's hour estimate was before the dimensions were added.' She isn't pressing; she's giving you the window before it closes.

Farren is already writing. The fourth sheet is half-full. He needs the corridor pace-count and the handprint location relative to the column — the last two measurements that will seal the geometry stack and make it legible to a Plateau specialist. The Waystaff is warm in your grip, the reference anchor node cool against your palm, and Aevi has returned to the shoulder position it uses when it has decided something is right.

turns.summary: Corridor dimensions given, geometry document sealed — Maren offers to carry it to the midmorning runner.
turns.narration:
You give Farren what he needs in the order he needs it: corridor length first — a hundred and twelve paces from the Second-Step column to the threshold, left wall running closer than right by about a palm's width the whole way. Then the handprint — sixty-eight paces east-northeast of the column, roughly shoulder-height on the interference wall, small enough to be a seventeen-year-old's. Farren's marking-stick moves without pausing. He doesn't write the way a man records; he writes the way a man builds, each notation placed with the care of a joint that has to bear weight.

The fourth sheet fills. He sets the stick down once, picks it up, adds a marginal bracket linking the corridor's four-second breathing-interval to the valve-geometry you both mapped independently, and then sets the stick down for good. 'That binds it,' he says. He gathers all four sheets plus your bark-paper sketch — both faces — and aligns the stack against the workbench edge. His hands press it flat once, as though he's settling a seam. 'The geometry is complete. Plateau reader will have everything they need to understand what the valve implies and where it sits relative to the chamber.'

Maren turns from the cracked door. The morning light is fully up now, gold and specific on the floor between you. 'Counter-bell is forty minutes, perhaps less,' she says, and something in the flatness of it means she has been counting. 'I can carry it. I know the runner by name.' She looks at Farren, then at you — not asking permission, offering a division of labor. Aevi settles at your shoulder, neither bright nor dim, the steady attentive-witness register it holds when something is about to be decided.

The document is complete. The question now is whose hands it travels in — and whether there is anything to add before those hands close around it and walk out into the morning.

turns.summary: Cellaceron touches the Waystaff's valve-point and feels the holding-state — the moment before Standing Wave commits — then loses coherence. Farren names what they found.
turns.narration:
With the document sealed and Maren's offer hanging in the air, you find yourself doing what hands do when the mind won't stop: you close your fingers around the Waystaff's grip and let the residual chamber-frequency hum through the nodes. Not technique — more like listening with your bones. The lower nodes are set to the chamber's resting interval, Farren said. The upper nodes open-register. You try to feel what that means in practice, tracing the grain with your thumb the way he showed you, pressing gently inward at the valve-point.

Something shifts. The frequency doesn't intensify — it redirects, a sensation like water finding a new channel, the stored interval briefly cohering into something that presses outward along the staff's length rather than bleeding into the air. You feel it in your palm: not a push, not sound exactly, but structured energy with nowhere assigned to go, wanting direction. It is not the standing-wave you know how to make. It is something upstream of that — the same mechanism, earlier in the chain, before it commits to shattering or holding. For a half-second you think you could feed it somewhere, route it to the Waystaff's upper nodes, to your own field, to the room itself. Then the coherence frays. The frequency settles back into the wood as background patience.

Aevi brightens one careful degree — not cataloging, not novelty, the attentive-witness register it saves for something that nearly was. Farren looks up from the sealed stack. He watched. He doesn't explain what you found; he just says, quietly, 'You felt the holding-state.' It's not a question. 'Most people only discover it goes somewhere — not that there's a moment before that, when it hasn't decided yet.' He picks up the marking-stick, then sets it down again. 'That's not Standing Wave technique. That's the substrate.' He glances at Maren, then back to you. 'Worth revisiting when the girl is safe.'

The counter-bell is forty minutes away. The document is sealed. The overnight Priority Council relay traffic is still unnamed. Maren is still facing the room, waiting on your answer about the carry.

turns.summary: Maren takes the document to the runner; Cellaceron reaches the Harrow cottage and assesses Teva.
turns.narration:
You hand the sealed document to Maren with both hands — Harmonic courtesy, because it matters — and she takes it the same way, tucking it flat against her forearm before her coat closes over it. No ceremony. She's already turning for the door when Farren speaks: 'Runner's name is Cael. Tell him the Plateau priority flag is mine.' Maren nods once and goes, her footsteps quick and even on the terrace stone, swallowed by the morning.

Farren watches the door settle behind her and then looks at you the way a man looks at a problem he hasn't finished thinking about. 'The holding-state won't wait for you to have time for it,' he says. 'But neither will the girl.' He picks up his marking-stick and turns back to his workbench — not dismissal, just continuation. 'Go.'

The Lower Terrace opens wide under a sky that is full spring-cold and cloudlessly bright, the phononic crystal buildings humming their midday chord around you. Aevi drifts at your shoulder, one steady degree above resting — the attentive-witness register, neither urging nor waiting. The Harrow cottage is east, up the lane, close enough that you can make it and be back before the counter-bell if Teva doesn't need more than a look.

The cottage door opens on dim air and the smell of old wood and the residual sweetness of the dried beans you've been eating off. Teva is where she's been since you first found her: curled near the right wall, breathing slowly and evenly. Four nights now. Her color is not wrong — not pale or gray — but there's a stillness to her that sits just outside ordinary sleep, a quality like held breath. The chamber's frequency, faint and four-second, moves through the Waystaff's nodes against your back.

turns.summary: Aevi reads the nanite flow and names the transfer direction: Teva is giving, not receiving.
turns.narration:
You set the Waystaff against the wall, lower yourself to the floor a few feet from Teva, and do nothing. That is the whole of it. The cottage smells of old wood and dried bean-dust and the faint mineral note that has been here since the first morning — the chamber's frequency bleeding up through stone and soil, audible now to you in a way it wasn't a week ago.

Teva's breathing is slow and even: in for four seconds, a pause, out for four. Not sleep. Something older and more patient than sleep. Her face is relaxed in the way faces are when the body has found a rhythm it trusts, and her left hand rests open on the floorboards as though she set something down and forgot to pick it up.

Aevi brightens without being asked. The teal motes drift off your shoulder and spread low across the room — not toward Teva directly, not yet, but mapping the air between you and her the way a cartographer maps coastline before committing to the interior. After a long minute the constellation shifts. Two motes settle near Teva's collarbone. The rest hold at the midpoint of the room, and their light has the particular quality you've learned to read as careful attention rather than novelty: steady, even, measuring. Aevi makes no sound. The hum it sometimes produces when thinking is absent — this is beyond thinking, this is reading.

Then, quietly: 'The direction is outward.' Three words, delivered close to your ear with the care of someone setting a glass on an unsteady surface. 'She is giving something to the chamber. Not the other way.'

turns.summary: Waystaff valve-point used to introduce a dissonant half-beat — Teva's breathing breaks from the chamber's four-second rhythm for the first time in four days.
turns.narration:
You bring the Waystaff down from the wall slowly, as though sudden movement might startle the rhythm itself. The grip-end nodes pulse warm against your palm — native Harmonic frequency, Farren's voice still in your ear — and you settle cross-legged at Teva's side, close enough that the cottage's four-second floor-pulse is louder than your own heartbeat.

The valve-point. You find it by feel now, the coolness offset in the grain two-thirds down from the grip, and you press your thumb there the way you pressed it at Farren's workbench. Not Standing Wave — not a push. The holding-state: the moment before a decision, the water finding the lip before it falls. You breathe in for four seconds with the room, then out, matching Teva's rhythm once, twice, three times — and on the fourth exhale you let the upper nodes open just a fraction off-interval. A half-beat early. A small, deliberate wrongness.

The four-second pulse stutters. Not violently — it's more like a clock whose pendulum catches on something and slows for one swing before resuming. Teva's breathing hitches. Her left hand closes on the floorboard, knuckles briefly white, and then her chest rises on a different beat — her own, ragged and uneven and entirely hers. She doesn't wake. But the synchronization has cracked. The chamber's rhythm continues in the floorboards, patient and unconcerned, but it is no longer the only thing in Teva's lungs.

Aevi drifts in — two motes that were stationed near her collarbone rise and circle once, settle again. The constellation doesn't dim. It waits. The thread is loose now; what you do in the next minutes will determine whether it frays free or pulls tight again.

turns.summary: Cellaceron holds the dissonant half-beat steady through the Waystaff until Teva's breathing breaks free of the chamber's rhythm and she wakes on her own.
turns.narration:
You hold the thumb-pressure steady on the valve-point and breathe against the cottage's four-second pulse instead of with it — a deliberate counter-rhythm, small and persistent. The Waystaff's grip-end nodes warm another degree. The dissonant half-beat you introduced doesn't sharpen into something harsh; it widens, a slow wedge worked into old timber. The floor-pulse continues its deep in-and-out, but Teva's breathing no longer locks to it. In for three seconds. A long, searching pause. Out for five.

She stirs before she wakes — a small event, the left hand unclosing and pressing flat against the floorboards as though testing whether the ground is still there. Her exhale breaks into a shudder that isn't distress so much as the body remembering it has edges. The synchronization doesn't shatter; it loosens, like a rope gone slack, still coiled but no longer taut. The four-second pulse continues in the stone beneath you, patient and unchanged — the chamber is still there, still breathing — but Teva's chest now rises on its own schedule.

Aevi shifts. The two motes that had risen toward her collarbone drift back to the shoulder-constellation, settling lower, the register dimming from watchful to something quieter. Not resolution — but the immediate alarm releasing its grip. The change is real and the girl is still here.

Teva's eyes open. Slow, unfocused, the way eyes open after deep water rather than sleep. She finds the ceiling first, then the shuttered window's edge of light, then you. Her voice, when it comes, is dry and thin: 'How long?'

turns.summary: Teva drinks, eats a little, and receives the four-night count — her voice back, her focus returning.
turns.narration:
You uncap the Waterskin and offer it without ceremony — just hold it out at the level of her eyeline and wait. Teva takes it with both hands, the way people do when they are not quite sure their hands will obey, and drinks in slow careful pulls. You watch her color come back. Four nights is a long time. Her lips are dry and the skin around her eyes has the drawn look of someone who has been fed by something that is not food.

While she drinks you break the dried bean-cake from your pack, splitting the heel into thirds and setting two pieces on the floorboards between you. She takes one after a moment, holds it, does not eat yet — but she holds it, which is something. The Waystaff leans against the wall behind you. You have not put it away. You are not sure why.

Aevi drifts forward from the shoulder-position to hover at the middle distance between you and Teva, motes settling into a low steady pattern — not the novelty-brightness, not the watchful alarm-register. Something quieter. Attendance, maybe. Teva notices the constellation and stares at it for a long breath, and some of the unfocus leaves her eyes. 'How long,' she says again, and this time it is less a question than a test of her own voice — checking that it still works.

You tell her: four nights in the chamber, one night here in the cottage, the morning now coming through the shutters. You say it plainly, no softening. She deserves the count. She takes it the way a person takes bad arithmetic — not with feeling, not yet, just filing it away for later. She eats the piece of bean-cake.

turns.summary: Teva hears the full account — chamber, pulse, Farren's document, the Plateau runner — and surfaces her first real sentence.
turns.narration:
You tell her plainly, the way you would want to be told. The chamber is still there. The four-second pulse in the floor — the one she can feel if she presses her palm flat — is the same frequency she synchronized with. It hasn't stopped, and you don't know yet what that means. You tell her she was gone four nights, that someone found the handprint, that a staff was built specifically to reach her. That it worked.

She listens with the bean-cake held between both hands and doesn't interrupt. Her eyes move to the floor when you mention the pulse. Her jaw tightens just slightly — not denial, recognition. She already knows the feeling underfoot. She's been living inside it for four days and part of her is still calibrated to it.

When you tell her about the document — Farren, the corridor geometry, Maren running to catch the midmorning runner, the Plateau priority flag — something shifts in her face. Not relief exactly. More like the vertigo of realizing a search party was out. 'Farren,' she says, the first full word beyond monosyllables, and it lands like she is checking whether the name still belongs to the same world she left. She looks at the Waystaff leaning against the wall and is quiet for a long moment. Aevi drifts a degree closer and dims to near-nothing — respectful, not absent.

'I wasn't lost,' she says, carefully, like someone testing whether it is true. 'I could hear it from the practice niches. Nobody else seemed to — ' She stops. Looks at you. 'You heard it too, didn't you. Or something heard it for you.' Her eyes find the barely-visible constellation of teal motes. Then back to you, waiting.

turns.summary: Cellaceron names Aevi plainly; Teva and the motes share a moment of mutual recognition — she is not the only one who heard the chamber's call.
turns.narration:
You tell her the truth about Aevi the way it was told to you — which is to say, not entirely. 'I don't know what it is, exactly,' you say. 'Old. Older than the Heights, I think. It reads nanite fields the way you read tone — not by effort, by nature. It noticed the chamber frequency before I understood what I was hearing.' You glance at the motes, and Aevi brightens one careful degree, as if acknowledging it's being introduced rather than described. Teva watches that brightening with the focused stillness of someone who has been listening to things other people couldn't hear her whole life and knows exactly what that quality of attention looks like.

She says, 'It heard it from out here.' Not a question. She looks at Aevi directly — not at you, not past you — the way a person looks at another person across a table. 'The frequency in the practice niches. I thought it was mine. Something in my own head going sideways.' A pause. She pulls in a slow breath, unrhythmed, still hers. 'It wasn't just mine.'

Aevi drifts forward one small increment. No words. The teal motes hold steady — brighter than attendance, quieter than alarm. Teva watches that forward movement and something in her face releases. Not relief exactly. More like: a long, careful hypothesis confirmed.

The floor hums its slow four-second in-and-out beneath you both. The chamber hasn't stopped. But Teva's breathing is her own now, and the question she asked — *you heard it too, didn't you* — has its answer sitting warm and teal between you, drifting patient as a lantern.

turns.summary: Teva finishes the bean-cake and waterskin; presses palms to the floor to test the residual pulse; declares herself ready to be questioned.
turns.narration:
You hold up a hand — not quite stopping her, just slowing the current. 'Finish the bean-cake first,' you say, and your tone lands somewhere between instruction and mercy. 'Twenty minutes. That's all you've been awake.' Teva blinks, then looks down at the heel of cake in her hands as if she'd forgotten it was there. She takes a bite. Then another. The focus doesn't leave her face but it changes quality — less braced, more present.

Aevi dims to a low, patient glow and drifts back a hand's width, giving the room air. The four-second pulse continues underfoot, steady as breathing, entirely indifferent to the fact that its hold on Teva has been broken. The shutters throw clean morning lines across the floor. Outside, somewhere up the terrace, a single wind chime adjusts itself with a sound like a thought coming clear.

Teva eats the whole piece. She drains the last inch of the waterskin. Then she sets both down and presses her palms flat against the floorboards — not seeking, just feeling. Testing whether the pulse lands differently now that she is not inside it. Whatever she finds, she doesn't say yet. When she looks up, her eyes are steadier than they were ten minutes ago, and something in the set of her mouth suggests she's made a private decision to be honest.

'Alright,' she says, quietly. 'Ask what you need to ask.'

turns.summary: Keen Appraisal finds the chamber's ghost mark: Teva's breathing still echoes the four-second interval, imprecise and fading, but present in the muscle.
turns.narration:
You let the room settle — Teva pressing her palms to the floor, morning light crossing the boards in clean bars — and let Keen Appraisal run the way you would over a piece of work you haven't examined closely yet. Not judgment. Just the habit of noticing.

The obvious details resolve first and tell you nothing new: the hollowness of four days without food, the careful way she's holding herself upright, the faint tremor in her left hand that you already knew about. Then the appraisal finds what it was looking for — the one thing out of place.

It's her breathing. Not the rhythm — that's hers again, irregular and alive, entirely free of the chamber's four-second lock. It's the *depth*. Every fourth breath, without any regularity she'd notice consciously, she takes fractionally more air than the breath before it. Not quite four-second — closer to four-point-three. A ghost of the interval, imprecise and fading, still caught in muscle memory. Whatever the chamber did to her, it ran deep enough that her body is still completing the pattern on its own, working it out of the system like a heard melody that won't stop repeating.

Aevi is already aware of it. You can tell by the way two motes have drifted an inch closer without brightening — attending, not alarmed. The chamber hasn't held her; she isn't synchronized anymore. But it has been in her long enough to leave a groove. Whether that groove is just a passing residue or something the chamber *intended* to leave — that's the question your appraisal names cleanly and cannot answer.

turns.summary: Resonant Sight confirms the breathing echo is nanite-structured, not muscular — the chamber placed a mark in Teva's tissue, fading but deliberate.
turns.narration:
You bring the Waystaff across your knees and let the two reads braid together the way you've learned to — the staff's lattice humming the chamber's reference tone while Prism Sight opens the heat-register behind your eyes. Resonant Sight isn't violent work, but it isn't gentle either: the field finds Teva and maps her the way it would map a wall — by how she rings and refracts at once.

What comes back is clear enough. The breathing echo isn't purely muscular. There's a thread — thin, not aggressive, the way a wire stays warm long after the current is cut. A trace of structured nanite patterning in the soft tissue around her diaphragm and throat, spaced at the chamber's interval. Not enough to be a hold. Not random either. Aevi's two forward motes drift a precise half-inch closer and go very still, which is the closest it comes to underlining something without speaking.

Teva watches your face while you read her. She's learned, in some four days you weren't present for, to read the face of someone who is listening to something she can't quite hear herself. 'Well?' she says. Her voice is steady.

'Not just habit,' you tell her. 'The chamber left a mark. Nanite-patterned, matched to its interval. It's fading — but it was placed.' You choose the word carefully. Aevi says nothing. Outside, the morning light has shifted another quarter-inch across the floorboards, and the four-second pulse in the boards themselves continues, utterly uninterested in the conversation happening above it.

turns.summary: Teva accepts Cellaceron's offer to travel together — practical and considered, not impulsive.
turns.narration:
The question lands simply — no ceremony, just the practical shape of it. Teva looks at you for a long moment, the way someone does when they're checking whether a thing is real. Then something in her shoulders settles, a fraction at a time, like a note finding its proper pitch.

'Yes,' she says. Not impulsive. Considered. 'I wasn't going to ask. I didn't think I'd earned it.' She looks down at her palms, still faintly warm from the floor's four-second pulse. 'But I want to understand what the chamber did. I want to be there when someone figures it out.'

Aevi brightens one careful degree — not the novelty-flare of something new, but the warmer register it holds when something fits correctly. It drifts a half-inch closer to Teva and then stops, a quiet endorsement.

There are loose ends to settle before you leave the cottage: the Harrow key to return or hold, the chamber mark in Teva's tissue still unresolved, the overnight Priority Council traffic Maren flagged, and the chamber itself still breathing four beats below the floorboards — waiting, indifferent, changed by nothing that's happened this morning.

turns.summary: Departure packed, Maren's backing secured — Teva travels under informal survey compact with Warden Council access preserved.
turns.narration:
You move through departure the way you've learned to move through most practical problems: hands first, thinking second. The Waystaff goes into the crook of your arm. The beeswax tapers come off the window ledge and wrap into the pack. Lamp oil, oilcloth square, cordage coil — each item named and seated. Teva watches without being asked, then starts gathering her own few things from the corner without being told to, which is the first sign that she'll be easy to travel with. Aevi drifts to the center of the room and dims to a steady watch-glow, patient as a held breath.

Maren finds you as you're locking the cottage door, or close enough to it — she's come up from the waystation the same road, still tucking her warden sash after the chill of the early walk. She sees Teva and stops. Not alarm, not protest. She reads the room the way a warden reads a chord — a full breath taken to hear all of it before she names a note.

You don't dress it up. Teva is the chamber's primary witness, the nanite mark is still active in her tissue, and the document Maren just carried to Cael is incomplete without a first-person account. Taking her back to the Disputed Zone without the party is worse than the alternative. Maren listens the way Elder Senna listens — on her feet, cup absent, watching your face. When you finish she's quiet for three breaths. Then: 'The Warden Council will want to speak with her.' Not a refusal. A condition. 'She comes with you, but she doesn't disappear into this. When Farren's work generates a formal inquiry, she's available to answer it.' She looks at Teva directly. 'Your choice. I won't make it for you.' Teva nods once, steady.

Maren turns back to you, the condition met. 'Then you have my backing — informally, for now. I'll note in the log that she's traveling under a survey compact and not at large.' She glances at the cottage key in your hand. 'Lock it. Leave it at the waystation desk on your way out. They'll hold it for Senna.'

turns.summary: Key returned at waystation desk; runner confirms three overnight Priority Heights internal packets — still no Plateau reply.
turns.narration:
The waystation desk sits just off the main terrace lane — a low counter under a carved phononic-crystal arch that hums at a frequency you feel more in your sternum than your ears. The morning runner isn't Cael; it's a different face, a young man with ink on his collar who looks up from the ledger as you approach. You set the Harrow cottage key on the counter — copper ring, worn grip — and name it for what it is: Senna's key, return to her when she comes through.

He takes it with both hands and drops it into a labeled clay dish behind the counter, no ceremony. 'Senna's hold, east terrace — I'll log it.' Then you ask the second question, and his demeanor shifts just slightly — not suspicion, but the careful precision of someone who has been thinking about what to volunteer.

'Upper-fork relay from the Plateau — nothing inbound yet. That's not unusual, the elevation route runs slow in early spring.' He pauses, then adds, as if continuing a thought that was already in motion: 'But there was another packet came down this morning on the Priority Council fork. Not Plateau — Heights internal. Third one since the night bell. Same flag on all three.' He taps the counter once, professionally. 'I'm not reading priority traffic. But you're traveling with a Warden compact, so.' He lets that sit.

Aevi, at your shoulder, goes very still — not the dim-and-withdraw of discomfort, but the bright-focused stillness it holds when something resolves into a shape worth examining. Teva, half a step behind, watches the runner with the measured attention of someone who grew up in a city where walls carry sound.

turns.summary: Runner reads the overnight packet pattern: three copies, no stagger, third seal heavier — Council-internal, possibly opening a formal inquiry.
turns.narration:
You keep it oblique — just a Warden-compact traveler checking relevance before heading east. The runner glances down at his ledger, then back up, making the same small calculation you'd make in his place: you're not asking for content, you're asking for shape.

'I can't read them,' he says, which is honest. 'But the flag pattern — three copies, same seal, same night, no stagger on the send-times — that's not routine inquiry correspondence. Routine inquiry goes single copy, confirms receipt.' He pauses, smoothing one corner of the ledger with his thumb. 'Zone traffic tends to come in pairs, mirrored for the two warden posts. Three feels like Council-internal. Could be they're moving to open something formally.' He leaves the something deliberately unspecified — he's not reading content, just pattern.

Behind you, Teva shifts her weight. She doesn't speak, but you catch the small intake of breath — the sound of someone connecting two pieces of information they'd been holding separately. Aevi goes very still at your shoulder, bright and non-alarmed, the way it gets when it's waiting to see where a thread leads.

The runner adds one more thing, quietly, as he closes the ledger: 'The third packet — seal was a different weight. Heavier press. That one didn't come from the same desk as the other two.'

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 8: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.summary: Waterskin refilled, bark-paper and four spare resonance crystals acquired on Maren's warden surety — party resupplied and ready to move.
turns.narration:
The morning runner nods before you finish the sentence. Waterskin first — he takes it through a low hatch and you hear the clean sound of a cistern tap, the Heights' water drawn from a sealed high-terrace source well away from the valley floor's troubles. He returns it full and stoppered, a small courtesy.

Paper is a moment's hesitation. 'Bark-press or crystal-sheet?' You say bark-press — cheaper, and you're not writing anything the Heights needs to read twice. He sets three sheets on the counter, folded once. They're the same dense weave as the bulletin notices: takes ink, takes charcoal, holds its shape damp. Useful.

The resonance crystals take longer. He disappears into the back and comes out with a small linen square folded into a packet — four nodes, thumbnail-sized, already facet-cut but untuned. 'Warden-store grade,' he says. 'Not the fine stuff from the upper workshops, but they'll seat into a staff frame.' You turn one over in your fingers. They're cool and slightly asymmetric, which is honest craftsmanship at this grade — the asymmetry is what makes them tunable rather than fixed. You slip them into the Traveler's Pack alongside the existing kit.

When you name Maren as surety, the runner's expression doesn't flicker — he simply opens the ledger to a fresh column, writes the warden's name and sash number, notes the three items, and slides it across for your mark. Teva watches the transaction from her half-step behind you with the same quiet attention she's been giving everything this morning, taking inventory of how things work here. Aevi brightens a single degree at the crystals — interest, not alarm — and goes still again. The thread on the three overnight Council packets hangs unanswered in the arch's hum. Whatever they're opening, they haven't told the desk runner, and he's been courteous enough not to speculate further.

turns.summary: Party departs east along the Lower Terrace lane toward the Disputed Zone boundary — Teva breaks her silence.
turns.narration:
The terrace lane runs east in clean morning light, phononic crystal paving stones humming underfoot at a frequency just below awareness — you feel it in your arches, a steady reminder that the Heights is always listening to itself. The air is cool, early-spring sharp, carrying the green smell of the valley floor rising from below. Aevi drifts at your left shoulder, a faint teal scatter against the bright sky, brightening slightly as you pass a wind-chime cluster the size of a barn — the chimes are tuned to the upper register, and for a moment the whole lane fills with a clean descending chord that settles something in your chest.

Teva walks half a step to your right, her Waystaff — your Waystaff, now in her hands for the moment while you take a cleaner grip on the pack — held crosswise in both arms the way a warden apprentice learns to carry a staff through public space. She hasn't said much since the waystation desk, but her eyes are moving: reading the notice posts, the terrace geometry, the tilt of the chime arrays. Taking inventory. You recognize the look — the same careful cataloging she did in the cottage with the floorboards.

The Lower Terrace thins out quickly at the east end. The high-density crystal buildings give way to spaced-out practice niches cut into the terrace wall, most of them empty at this hour — just the echo of someone's scale exercise drifting down from an upper level, badly timed. The lane narrows and starts to slope, the paving stones grading from cut crystal to rough-dressed fieldstone, and ahead the terraced Heights gives way to the first scramble of the descent path into the Disputed Zone. The boundary isn't marked, but you can feel it: the sternum-hum from the Heights drops in register as you walk, and somewhere ahead there's a faint shimmer at the edge of the light where the two fields begin to interfere.

Teva breaks her silence just before the last chime cluster: 'The practice niches.' She doesn't phrase it as a question. 'Is that where the Council packets were routed, or further up?' She's not asking about the packets — she's asking something underneath that, about who already knew what she found.

turns.summary: Resonant Sight finds a ghost of the chamber's four-second frequency pressed into the practice niche crystal — the signal traveled outward on its own.
turns.narration:
You stop at the last niche — a shallow scoop cut into the east terrace wall, crystal-lined, sized for one apprentice to sit cross-legged and practice in isolation. The surface is clean. Nobody has used it this morning.

You set one hand against the crystal and let the Waystaff's third node — the anchor, Farren's reference point — sit close without touching. Resonant Sight opens slowly here, the two fields braiding in the way they've learned to: vibration first, reading the crystal's grain, then prism-sense sliding in along the same channel, reading how the lattice refracts whatever it holds. The niche is quiet. But not empty. Somewhere in the middle register, below the Heights' maintained hum, there is a faint structural ghost — a four-second rhythm pressed into the crystal's memory like a thumbprint in warm wax. Not the Heights' frequency. Slower. The chamber's.

The niche didn't generate the signal. It received it, the way a bell-jar holds a pitch after the tuning fork is removed. You pull back, and Teva, who has been watching your hand and not your face, says quietly: 'All three of them?' You don't have to check the other two niches. The ghost in this one is faint but unmistakable — and this is the furthest from the Zone boundary. If it reached this far, it reached all three. Which means the signal didn't require a person to carry it. It went looking.

Aevi has gone still at your shoulder — not the dim withdrawal of discomfort, but the bright, focused stillness it uses when it is tasting something in the air and not yet ready to name it.

turns.summary: Teva's full four-night account extracted — she was not lost but called, and her structural read of the frequency matched the chamber's own geometry before she ever crossed the threshold.
turns.narration:
You answer Teva's question first — 'All three, yes, but not the same way. The nearest one is carrying a ghost of the frequency you followed. The other two are clean.' — and then you keep walking, because the descent path is ahead and the Disputed Zone boundary has a way of interrupting conversations at the worst possible moment. 'Tell me from the beginning,' you say. 'When you first heard it in the niches. Not what you decided to do about it — just the moment it arrived.'

She talks. And once she starts, she doesn't stop.

The frequency came four nights ago — late evening practice, second niche from the east, the one she preferred for resonance-threading work because the left wall was slightly off-square and gave her honest feedback. She'd been running a simple standing-wave exercise, the kind you repeat until your hands know it without your mind. And then, underneath the exercise's return signal, something else: a pulse at a rhythm she didn't recognize. Not dissonant. Not loud. Patient. She said it felt like someone knocking on a door from the other side of a very long hall. She sat with it for two hours, trying to locate the source by triangulating across all three niches, moving between them in the dark after the evening bell. She had told no one — not because she was hiding it, she says carefully, but because she had no language for it yet. In the Heights, you don't report a frequency until you can name it.

Here is what the critical read gives you that a simple account never would: the precise sensory vocabulary she uses is not apprentice language. When she describes the pulse's character she says *anchored, not ambient* — a warden phrase, the kind that comes from two years of threshold-work she hasn't formally done yet. She heard more than the frequency. She heard its *structure*. Somewhere in those two hours of triangulation she was reading the shape of the thing without knowing she was doing it, and what she found matched what you found in the chamber: a hemispherical acoustic envelope, self-referential, measuring its own boundaries. She didn't know that yet. But she'd felt it. She followed it because she was already, in some deep practical sense, *inside* it. Aevi, beside your shoulder, goes very still and very bright — the third register, not alarm, not novelty. Something like recognition.

By the time the descent path begins beneath your boots — cut crystal giving way to fieldstone, the phononic hum dropping a full register as the interference boundary approaches — you have her complete account, and it tells you something the warden notice never could: Teva didn't get lost chasing a sound. She got *called*, and she answered with the skill of someone years ahead of her rank, and the chamber knew exactly what it was receiving.

turns.summary: Teva traces 'anchored, not ambient' to Farren's open-door lectures — she used it precisely and didn't know how precisely.
turns.narration:
The fieldstone path drops another step and the phononic hum fades to something you feel only in your back teeth now — the Heights releasing its hold as the Disputed Zone boundary draws nearer. You let the question arrive without preamble, the way Maren would. 'Anchored, not ambient — that's warden survey language. Formal calibration vocabulary. Where did you pick that up?'

Teva doesn't answer immediately. She shifts the Waystaff in both arms, the grip-end nodes catching a thread of morning light, and you watch her work through whether the question is an accusation. It isn't, and she seems to decide that. 'Farren,' she says, and the name lands simply. 'Not directly. He runs the advanced geometry sessions on the Second Terrace — closed enrollment, but the door was open twice when I passed and he talks loudly when he's working through a problem. He uses that word specifically for something that has its own source rather than borrowing from the matrix. I started using it in my niche notes and nobody corrected me.' A small pause. 'I didn't realize it would sound wrong coming from a second-year apprentice.'

Aevi brightens one careful degree. Not alarm, not the novelty-register — something in between, the kind of attention it gives when a piece slots into a larger shape it hasn't named yet. Teva glances toward it sidelong, as she has been doing since the cottage, the small habitual check she's developed for the motes' read on a moment.

'It doesn't sound wrong,' you say. 'It sounds accurate. That's the thing.' Because 'anchored' is the precise word — not borrowed resonance, not sympathetic vibration, not field-echo from the Heights matrix. A source. The chamber has a source, and Teva named it correctly from outside its threshold, before she ever crossed, using language she half-heard through an open door. What you don't say, and don't need to yet: that the mark the chamber left in her tissue is the same architecture as an anchor. Placed. Deliberate. Patient.

turns.summary: Cellaceron tells Teva plainly about the nanite mark in her tissue — placed, patterned, still present; she receives it without breaking.
turns.narration:
The descent path levels briefly at a switchback — a natural pause, and you take it. Teva has the Waystaff crosswise in both arms still, grip-end toward you, and you match her pace so the next words land side by side rather than at her back.

'There's something else you should know,' you say. 'When I read you in the cottage — the deeper look, Resonant Sight, both fields together — I found a mark in your tissue. Diaphragm and throat. It follows the same four-second interval as the chamber's resting frequency. The pattern is too regular to be muscle memory. Too specific to be coincidental.' You let that settle before the word that matters most. 'Placed, Teva. Not incidentally. The chamber put it there deliberately, and it's still fading out.'

She stops walking. Not dramatically — she just stops, the way a person does when their feet have to decide whether the ground is still trustworthy. She looks down at the Waystaff nodes, then at the slope below, then at nothing in particular. Aevi holds very still at your left shoulder, brighter by the smallest increment — watching, not interpreting. 'It used my own interval,' she says, eventually. Not a question. She's placing the geometry of it, the same patient structural instinct that read the chamber's envelope before she ever crossed its threshold. You can see her not panicking, which is a choice she's making in real time.

'Is it still changing anything?' she asks. Her voice is level. She wants the honest answer, not the comfortable one.

turns.summary: Keen Appraisal reads the mark in open air — preparation, not command; fainter than two days ago; Waystaff and Teva briefly ring the same note.
turns.narration:
You let the switchback's natural quiet hold for a breath, then draw the Keen Appraisal up slowly — the 4-energy discipline settling into place like a lens finding focus. No niche crystal nearby, no Heights tone humming interference into the read. Just morning air and the faint interference shimmer at the tree line, and the mark itself.

It resolves differently out here. On the terrace, the Heights' maintained chord was loud enough that the mark read against it — a foreign signal against a known background. In open air, with the phononic field fading to almost nothing, it reads as itself. The pattern in Teva's diaphragm and throat is not aggressive. It is not a claim or a seal. It is closer to a tuning — the kind of mark a craftsman leaves to confirm that a surface will hold a specific frequency when called on. The chamber patterned her the way a resonance crystal is patterned before seating: prepared to receive, not commanded to act. The interval is fainter than it was in the cottage two days ago. Whatever the chamber started, it is either finishing on its own schedule or waiting for a condition that hasn't arrived yet.

Teva is watching your face with the careful attention of someone who has decided not to panic and is working to maintain that decision. 'Different,' she says. Not a question — she felt the read, faint as it was. The Waystaff's grip-end nodes carry a thread of the chamber's reference tone, and in the open air that thread and the mark in her tissue are suddenly, briefly audible to both of you as the same note.

Aevi brightens — not alarm, not novelty. The third register. It says nothing. But it is attending very closely to whatever just happened between the staff and Teva's breathing.

turns.summary: Teva names what she felt in the cottage: the chamber checking its own work every four seconds, even in her sleep.
turns.narration:
You hold your ground at the switchback and let the question sit in the air between you. 'You said your own interval. Before I told you anything about a mark.' Teva's eyes drop to the Waystaff's grip-end nodes, still faintly warm from that brief resonance, and she breathes out through her nose — not deflection, you read it as gathering. The morning is quiet enough that you can hear the difference between the Heights' fading hum and the earth-sound of the slope below. She heard the difference too. 'In the cottage,' she says. 'The first night. I kept waking at the same gap. Not from a sound. From the absence of one.' She adjusts her grip on the staff without looking up. 'Like something was counting and I was the number it kept landing on. Four seconds, give or take. I counted the ceiling beams to be sure I wasn't imagining it.' A pause. 'I was not imagining it.'

Aevi brightens a single careful degree at your left shoulder — not the alarm register, not even the recognition-flash from before. Something quieter. The motes hold their position without drifting, which is its own kind of attention.

Teva lifts her eyes then, and what you see there is not fear — it's the look of someone who has been carrying a precise and unwelcome observation for four days and has finally found a mouth for it. 'It wasn't waking me. It was — checking. Whether I was still tuned.' She says the last word like it surprises her. 'That's the right word, isn't it. The chamber was checking its own work.' Her grip on the Waystaff tightens fractionally. 'Every four seconds. Even in my sleep.'

The interference shimmer moves at the tree line below, slow and independent of the wind. You are twenty paces from packed earth and the descent into the Zone. You have the thing she was carrying — and she has named it herself, which matters more than if you'd pried it out. What you do with that knowledge in the next few steps will shape what she's willing to say once the phononic field is gone entirely.

turns.summary: Aevi names what the mark's open-air behavior means: preparation is not passive — the frequency must arrive, or she must be moved toward it. The Waystaff may already be part of the channel.
turns.narration:
The switchback holds its quiet. You turn your head left — not to speak to the air, but to Aevi, the way you've learned: a small shift of attention, an opening. The motes brighten one degree, then another, moving from the third register to something warmer and more considered. They drift forward slightly, until they're level with the Waystaff's grip-end nodes rather than your shoulder — attending the staff and Teva in the same glance.

'The mark is patient,' Aevi says, from just behind your ear. 'Preparation, as you read it. But preparation is not passive.' A pause — the motes hold very still, the way they do when the next word costs something. 'What is being prepared for must arrive. Either the frequency comes to her, or she is moved to a place where she can receive it.' The voice is quieter than usual, pitched below the morning air. 'The staff rang the same note. That was not coincidence. That was a test the mark ran — checking whether the channel exists yet.'

Teva has gone still with the Waystaff crosswise in her arms. She heard. Her eyes track to the motes, then to the grip-end nodes, then back to you — not frightened, you read it as someone filing a piece into place that changes the shape of everything around it. 'A channel,' she says, flat and precise. 'The staff is part of what the mark is waiting for.'

Aevi brightens one final careful degree and says nothing more. The interference shimmer at the tree line moves slow and independent below you, and the packed earth of the Disputed Zone's margin is ten paces ahead.

turns.summary: Solar crystal charge on the descent shelf — three nodes fully loaded, one shallow; provisions eaten, party rested at the margin of the Disputed Zone.
turns.narration:
The descent path opens up as the fieldstone gives way to packed earth — you stop at a wide southern-facing shelf where the slope drops away and the sky is unobstructed all the way to the noon meridian. The interference shimmer is visible below, slow and silver at the tree line, but up here the light is clean and falls without interruption. You turn a slow quarter-circle, reading the angles with Prism Sight's passive warmth-register, and the shelf confirms what you suspected: best solar exposure on this whole terrace face, no overhanging stone, no phononic crystal close enough to bleed frequency into the charging lattice. You unshoulder the Traveler's Pack and set it on a flat rock. 'Here,' you say, and Teva — who has been watching how you move since the switchback — doesn't ask why.

You direct her to the pack: mess kit, Waterskin, the Greenlore forage you've been carrying. She moves without complaint, spreading the kit on the warm stone and working through the provisions with the methodical attention of someone who needs something physical to do while her mind is elsewhere. Aevi drifts toward the southern edge of the shelf, brightened faintly, attending the interference layer below — keeping watch without being asked. You draw the four untuned resonance crystals from the inventory pouch, seat them one by one in the Waystaff's open node sockets, and angle the grip-end nodes directly into the column of noon light.

The charging is not effortless. The nodes are warden-store grade — slightly asymmetric, facets cut for tunability rather than fixed alignment — and the first crystal takes almost twenty minutes before it catches, the light moving through the lattice in fits rather than a smooth wash. You adjust the angle twice, then a third time, and feel the moment it finds the resonant channel: a low warmth in your palm that isn't from the sun, and a faint broadening of the photoluminescent register in Prism Sight's edge-vision. One by one, three of the four crystals take the charge and hold it — the fourth loads shallow, catches something and then releases it, and you feel the subtle slip in your grip like a note resolving flat. Not a full charge. Two-thirds, maybe. Enough for the field to read as loaded, not enough to trust it at full draw. You pocket that crystal separately. Teva, who has been watching the last few minutes, holds out the Waystaff without being asked so you can seat the charged nodes back along the grain-line. Her hands are steadier than they were on the switchback.

By the time the pack is repacked and the kit stowed, the noon light has moved past its peak. You've eaten — the provisions Teva laid out were spartan but adequate — and you're sitting on the flat stone with the Waystaff across your knees and the valley spread below: the interference shimmer just downslope, the Heights' terrace-hum barely a memory at your back, the Disputed Zone margin close enough now that you can see where the light begins to bend. Aevi settles near your left shoulder, back to the familiar third register, motes slow and content. Whatever comes next, you are not walking into it empty.

turns.summary: Party descends the terrace path toward the Disputed Zone — herbs gathered, Teva assessed, shimmer-edge reached.
turns.narration:
The shelf gives way to packed-earth switchbacks almost immediately — the descent path is steeper than it looks from above, the soil loose and rust-colored between exposed root-runs, and the interference shimmer at the tree line is no longer a feature on the horizon but a destination that grows with every step. You lead, Waystaff in hand, setting a brisk pace. Teva falls in behind without prompting, and within a dozen paces you have your read of her: she moves well. Not trained-well, not the smooth economy of someone who's been drilled, but naturally well — long legs covering ground efficiently, weight centered, feet reading the slope without conscious thought. She's seventeen but carries herself a couple of years older, lean in the way of someone who spends time outdoors and forgets to eat when focused. Her hair is dark and close-cropped on one side, longer on the other — an asymmetry that reads as practical rather than deliberate, the way people look when they've been cutting their own hair by feel. Her face is angular and attentive, with the kind of stillness in the brow that means she's always running a secondary calculation. When she catches you watching, she doesn't look away — she meets it briefly and files it.

You watch the path edges as you descend, the eye moving between footing and herb-sign with the practiced split-attention of someone who's done this kind of walking often. The slope is early-spring dry on top, but there's moisture running in the root-shadow — you catch the broad, silver-margined leaves of lungwort pressed flat against an outcrop, and a low sprawl of ground ivy at the path's edge where the shade begins. Not remarkable finds, but reliable: lungwort for chest tightness, ground ivy for inflammation. You pinch a sprig of each and tuck them into the Traveler's Pack's outer pocket without breaking stride, noting the quantity mentally. Not enough for a poultice yet, but a start. Deeper in the tree line, where the shimmer begins to bleed color into shadow, you might find more — or you might find that the interference has leached the local plants into something you don't recognize.

Teva speaks without preamble, about twenty paces from the shimmer's edge. 'The corridor sounds different from outside than from in.' She's not asking — she's indexing aloud, the way Farren probably taught without meaning to. 'From here it feels like the slope is listening.' She pauses, one foot on a root-crossing. 'I don't mean that poetically.' Aevi drifts forward from your shoulder to approximately the Waystaff's midpoint, motes brightening in the slow attentive pattern that means it is tasting the air without comment — the interference layer is close enough now to register. The shimmer is five or six paces ahead: slow, silver-edged, and very quiet, the way things get right before they aren't.

turns.summary: Party crosses the shimmer into the Disputed Zone; Teva maps echo-lag on the fly; Aevi stills on something in the interference layer.
turns.narration:
You step forward without ceremony. The shimmer receives you the way it always does — not like a door opening, but like a room remembering you were here before. The silver-edged interference is cool against your face for half a breath, the Waystaff's nodes humming a single collective note as you cross, and then the tree line is behind you and the Disputed Zone opens on all sides: low twisted growth, soil that holds two colors at once, light that arrives from slightly the wrong angle for the shadows it casts.

Teva follows one stride behind. You hear her breath catch — not fear, more like the catch of someone recognizing a smell from a long time ago. She doesn't slow. Behind her, Aevi drifts through the shimmer without pause, the motes shifting from their attentive teal to something fractionally cooler, fractionally brighter, as the interference field registers around them. They settle near the Waystaff's grip-end and stay there.

The path ahead is known. Loose shale on the left, root-runs doubling where the interference-pressure warps growth, and somewhere ahead the narrow entrance to the shaped corridor with its breathing four-second frequency and its palm-width asymmetry you can pace from memory. The light here does what it always does: it works a moment behind the sound, so that your footsteps arrive at your ears a beat before the visual confirmation of the stones you've stepped on. Teva murmurs, low and precise: 'The echo comes after the light, not after the step.' She's already mapping it.

Aevi brightens a single deliberate degree, then stills. Something in the interference layer is registering — not alarm, not novelty. Recognition.

turns.summary: Cellaceron steps between Teva and the corridor entrance; Aevi holds still at the grip-end, reading.
turns.narration:
You shift your grip on the Waystaff and step half to the side, putting your body between Teva and the corridor entrance ahead. "Get behind me," you say — quiet, no alarm in it, just the flat clarity of someone arranging the board before the game moves. Teva reads it immediately and slips left without argument, one stride back and a pace off your shoulder, giving you a clear swing-arc if you need it. She doesn't ask why. The Zone has already told her enough.

The shaped corridor entrance is perhaps thirty paces ahead through the root-warped undergrowth — a dark seam in the hillside, precise where everything around it is ragged. Even from here you can feel the four-second interval in your teeth, not heard so much as noticed: a rhythmic absence where sound should continue. The Waystaff's nodes answer it in a single, low collective hum, the charged crystals warming fractionally against the interference field.

Aevi doesn't drift back to your shoulder. The motes remain at the Waystaff's grip-end, cooler and brighter than when you crossed the shimmer — a fractionally tighter constellation than usual, the kind of stillness that means attention fully spent. Whatever it registered when you crossed, it is still registering it. It hasn't named it yet.

turns.summary: Zone scouted, alcove camp established sixty paces from the corridor entrance; Teva tracking the four-second interval by lip-count; Aevi holding the berm-crest watch.
turns.narration:
You move through the thirty paces to the corridor entrance at a measured walk, Waystaff nodes reading the ground ahead of your feet. The scout unfolds over the better part of two hours — a slow, methodical arc through the Zone's nearest quarter-mile, never going so deep that the shimmer behind you drops below fifty paces. You work the two-field braid the way you've learned to: Prism Sight first, reading the heat-register of recent passage, recent touch; Sonic Resonance behind it, pressing standing queries into the root-warped walls and the rust-soil underfoot. The corridor entrance holds its shape like a nail in soft wood — everything around it bent by proximity, the undergrowth growing at angles that acknowledge it without naming it. You find two secondary alcoves flanking the approach, natural breaks in the hillside that the Zone's interference has widened into something almost deliberate: dry, roofed by overlapping root-mats, invisible from more than a dozen paces unless you know the four-second interval and can feel what sits at its source. You mark both in memory.

Teva says very little during the scout, but her silence has texture. She's counting. You can hear her lips moving at the four-second beat, tracking the interval the way a sailor tracks a lighthouse pulse — not for comfort, for bearing. Twice she raises a hand to indicate a direction she wants you to turn. Both times the Prism-Sonic braid confirms something ahead of your own read: once a section of corridor wall where recent heat-sign clings in a palm-wide handprint at shoulder height; once a stand of root-growth where the interference lag is stronger by half, the light a full beat behind its shadows, and the soil a shade darker with moisture that shouldn't be there at this elevation in early spring. She doesn't explain either. She doesn't have to.

The camp goes in at the better of the two alcoves — the northern one, a shallow dome of root-mat and compressed earth, shielded from the shimmer-wind by a low natural berm. It is not comfortable. The interference hum is present in the back teeth and in the joints of the hands, and the light, even in early evening, behaves wrong: it leans rather than falls. But the berm cuts the prevailing shimmer-draft, the root-mat overhead is dry, and the curve of the wall behind you is solid against the Zone's ambient tonal distortion. You set the Waystaff across the entry with its three charged nodes facing outward, unroll the bedroll from the Traveler's Pack, and take stock. Three crystals charged. One shallow. The corridor entrance is sixty paces south, visible as a dark seam through the undergrowth. The four-second interval is a constant companion now, not intrusive — the kind of background that becomes furniture.

Aevi drifts to the berm's crest without being asked and stills there, constellation tighter than it has been all day, motes slightly cooler, the teal shade deepening toward the blue end. It hasn't named what it recognized at the shimmer crossing. It is still reading it.

turns.summary: Light banked into a beeswax taper at the alcove camp — a conserved charge, enough for several hours underground.
turns.narration:
The evening light in the Disputed Zone arrives wrong — it bends around the interference layer and pools in low places where it has no business pooling, casting shadows that don't match their sources. But light is light, even here, and you know how to read it.

You reach into the Traveler's Pack and pull out one of the beeswax tapers — not to burn it, but to use the pale wax as a holding vessel. You've worked with stranger substrates. You angle the Waystaff's open-face lattice toward the last slant of evening coming through the canopy gap above the alcove, and you don't push. You just open a channel and let the light find its own way in, the way you'd cup your hands under a slow spring rather than divert the whole stream. The nodes hum at a low, private frequency — not the Zone's four-second interval, something older and calmer. The taper's surface takes on a faint interior warmth, a glow pressed down into the wax rather than sitting on top of it. It won't be bright. It will be enough: three hours of close work underground, maybe four if you're careful with the release.

Teva stops her lip-count for the length of a breath, watching the wax go warm. Then she resumes. Aevi doesn't move from the berm crest, but two motes brighten briefly — not alarm, something closer to acknowledgment. The alcove settles into its first real quiet of the evening, the berm cutting the worst of the interference draft, the Waystaff's nodes facing outward across the entry like patient sentinels.

turns.summary: Solo greenlore-and-sight scout reveals a forward alcove and a lateral moisture-flow band running toward the corridor — no threat, but the ground exhales something old and slow.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the berm edge and speak low enough that it doesn't carry past the alcove. "Going out alone — thirty, forty minutes. Greenlore sweep along the corridor approach, Resonant Sight where the ground reads wrong." You touch two fingers to the Waystaff's grip and slide it toward Teva. "Keep this across the entry. If anything moves with intent, the nodes will register before your ears do." Teva's lip-count doesn't stop, but she nods once and takes the staff with both hands, seating it the way you showed her. Aevi brightens a single mote — acknowledgment — then settles back to the berm crest. The watch continues without you.

You move out into the Zone dark with only the beeswax taper left unlit in your coat pocket and your hands free. The conservation principle holds here: no blaze of Prism Sight, no standing wave broad-cast. You open the field narrow and low, the way you'd hold a candle in wind — just enough to read what's already radiating. The interference layer bends the light-register sideways in places, which is where the location actually helps you: bent light collects in seams, and seams are where the ecology tells the truth. You're not pushing power into the ground. You're listening to what the ground is already doing.

The Greenlore thread comes up alongside it almost automatically — the two disciplines don't fight here the way they might in cleaner territory. You read bark-stress in a stand of young ash forty paces north: the cambium layer on the Zone-facing side is growing thicker, compensating for something. You read root-tip density in exposed soil at a crumbling bank — roots that should go down are going sideways, avoiding depth. And at the moisture-dark soil anomaly you'd flagged from the scout, Prism Sight catches a faint thermal differential: not warm, not cold exactly, but *wrong-temperature* in a band that runs roughly east-southeast toward the corridor's bearing. The ground here is releasing something so slow it doesn't register as heat — more like a long exhale the soil has been making for years.

No active threat. The Zone's nearest quarter-mile is empty of recent human passage beyond your own earlier prints. But the anomaly band is real and traceable, and twenty paces further along it you find a second alcove — smaller than your camp, no natural roof but three-sided stone and a dry earthen floor, sheltered from the shimmer-draft. It would serve as a forward rest point before the corridor. And at the base of the stone on the south face, growing in the gap between rock and root-mat where the thermal band passes through: a dense mat of lungwort, broad silver-margined leaves folded flat in the night cool, and beneath it a low sprawl of marsh-pennywort — not medicinal in the usual sense, but a reliable marker plant, one that only roots where subsurface moisture is moving laterally rather than draining down.

turns.summary: Greenlore surged in the interference layer — lungwort and ground ivy destroyed, hands empty, forearms resonance-burned.
turns.narration:
You move toward the root-shadow where you spotted the lungwort earlier — broad silver-margined leaves catching the wrong-pooled light, ground ivy threaded between the stones at the thermal band's margin. You crouch and open Greenlore, letting the nanite-field spread through your fingertips into the soil, reading root-tip tension and moisture-map and the slow chemistry of early-spring growth. Standard work. You've done it a hundred times on cleaner ground.

The Zone pushes back.

Not violently — nothing in the Disputed Zone is ever clean about what it does. The interference layer runs underneath the Greenlore field like a current under a ford, and when you press the read into the thermal band's margin, the two fields braid wrong. The Greenlore surges — you feel it spike through your palms and up your forearms, sudden and hot, reading everything at once: the lungwort's root-system, the ground ivy, the sub-thermal differential bleeding up through the stone gap, the moisture-dark soil six paces north, the corridor's exhalation coming up through the gap underneath it all. Too much, too fast. Your field doesn't pick what matters. It just pulls.

The lungwort tears free with too much root attached and comes apart in your hands — viable leaves, yes, but the delicate nodal structure that makes it useful for a poultice is shredded. The ground ivy fares worse: the surge snaps three runners at the crown, and what's left is young growth, barely established, not worth harvesting. You're left holding a fistful of damaged material and a burning ache that runs from your wrists to your elbows, the feedback of a field that bit back. Nothing to show but the sting. You stay crouched for a breath, pressing your palms flat against the cool stone until the resonance-burn fades to a dull warmth, and then you stand and turn back toward the alcove — hands empty, forearms tender, the thermal band exhaling its slow old breath behind you as though nothing happened at all.

turns.summary: Forward alcove yields liverwort and a scarred mark on the north stone face — not enough for a full poultice, but the ground didn't surge again.
turns.narration:
You pull back from the ruined root-patch, forearms still tender where the interference layer bit, and angle east-southeast toward the forward alcove rather than cutting straight back to camp. Twenty paces through the wrong-pooled light, the three-sided stone shelter comes into view — dry earthen floor, no natural roof but the stone walls cut the shimmer-draft enough that the air inside is almost still.

Something did grow here. You can see it by taper-light: a pale fringe of what might be shelf fungus at the base of the north-facing stone face, and — lower, where the floor meets the wall — a mat of liverwort, flat and waxy, the kind that doesn't care what the ground underneath is doing because it drinks air instead. Not what you were hoping for. Liverwort is better than nothing for drawing moisture from a poultice base, but it won't do the work that lungwort does — no silver-leaf volatile chemistry, no anti-inflammatory draw. You pinch a section free carefully, keeping your field well above the stone floor and well away from where the thermal band's margin runs. It doesn't surge. The alcove is just dry enough, just far enough from the band's path, that the interference layer here is only a background hum.

The shallow: you get the liverwort. You don't get enough. Maybe a thumb's width of mat — workable as a binder or a dressing base, nothing more. You fold it into the oilcloth square to keep it damp and tuck it into your belt. As you straighten, the taper-light catches a mark on the north face of the stone that the first scout pass missed entirely: a small scoring, about chest height on the inner wall face, two crossed lines with a third running diagonal through them. Not a warden notation — wrong form, wrong hand. Old. Your forearms give a low throb of warning and you don't press Prism Sight to read it, not tonight. You note the position in your mind: north face, inner wall, chest height.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 9: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.summary: Resonant Sight reads the forward alcove's scored mark — a survey cross corrected by an older diagonal in a different hand.
turns.narration:
You seat yourself against the south wall of the forward alcove, back to stone, taper balanced on a flat ledge where its light falls clean across the north face. The scored mark is chest-height and old — liverwort has been growing over its lower edge for what might be years.

You open the braid carefully. Not a push — a listen. Prism Sight first, reading the light that's pooled in the chisel-cuts and the lichen-shadow; then Sonic Resonance underneath it, a barely-voiced hum pressed into the stone through your palm, quiet enough that it doesn't carry past the alcove walls. The interference layer stirs at the contact but doesn't surge — the stone here sits on solid bedrock, not thermal drift, and the braid finds its footing.

What comes back is geometry. Two crossed lines are a survey notation in Harmonic convention — bearing-cross, two directions held in tension. The diagonal through them is older than the cross-marks; different hand, harder edge, cut with something sharper than a standard chisel. The stone under the diagonal reads dense and intentional. Not defacement, not warding — annotation. Someone corrected this mark, or completed it. The cross says 'two paths cross here.' The diagonal says 'neither is the right one.'

Aevi's motes are at the berm-crest, sixty paces west. You can't hear Teva's count. The taper burns warm and steady in the still air, and the mark sits in your sight like a problem waiting for the person who already half-knows the answer.

*✦ Aevi has changed — Kindled Chorus.*

turns.summary: Resonant Sight holds the diagonal's bearing — a third direction beneath the stone, running to intersect the corridor mid-span.
turns.narration:
You don't push deeper — you wait. The braid is already open, the stone already speaking; adding force here would be like shouting into a conversation already in progress. You let Prism Sight drift along the diagonal's groove while Sonic Resonance hums the barest sustain through your palm, just enough to keep the channel honest.

The interference layer obliges, in its way. The Disputed Zone has its own kind of order — not neat, but consistent in its strangeness, like a river that runs uphill but always uphill. The diagonal cut is older than the survey cross by at least a generation, maybe two. Under the stone at that bearing the braid finds not a void but a seam: a compressed change in material density, as if something was laid beneath the bedrock surface long before the alcove was carved, and the person who cut the diagonal knew it was there and was marking the direction it runs. Not toward the corridor. Not toward the shimmer edge. Somewhere between — a third bearing that neither the survey cross nor the shimmer path accounts for.

The braid doesn't tell you what's down there. It tells you the diagonal's author knew, and the survey team that came after either didn't, or chose not to say. You ease Sonic Resonance quiet first, then let Prism Sight follow — conserving the draw, no surge, the taper barely flickers. Your forearms are still tender from the earlier surge. You don't make them work harder than this moment requires.

The third bearing sits in your mind now like a sound heard once in a quiet room: northeast-by-east of the scored mark, running beneath the ground at a shallow angle that would intersect the corridor's path somewhere past the forty-pace mark. Not at the entrance. Not at the threshold. Somewhere in the middle.

turns.summary: Keen Appraisal on the diagonal cut choked in Zone interference — three temporal layers, no clean read, a nosebleed for the attempt.
turns.narration:
You press your fingertips into the diagonal groove and open Keen Appraisal — conserved, economical, the way you'd read a carpenter's old plane-marks to understand the hand that made them. The groove is deep enough to hold shadow. It should tell you something.

It tells you too much, all at once, and in the wrong order.

The Disputed Zone has been patient with you tonight — the Resonant Sight braid held clean, the stone cooperated. But Keen Appraisal reads *objects and their histories*, and this stone sits on an interference seam that doesn't distinguish between one era and the next the way a normal surface does. The moment your focus settles into the groove, the Zone's chaos-order axis flexes like a bridge too heavily loaded: the tool-impression fractures into at least three distinct temporal layers, each one overwriting the last in your perception rather than stacking cleanly beneath it. You get a flicker of something — a smaller hand than yours, a precise short stroke, confidence not haste — and then it drowns in interference noise that tastes like static and smells like struck iron.

You pull back sharply, and your nose bleeds. Just a slow trickle, but the message is plain: the Zone's own order swallowed your read whole, filed it somewhere you can't retrieve, and sent the feedback home through the nearest soft tissue available. You press the back of your wrist to your upper lip and sit with it for a moment. The taper burns steady. The mark says nothing further. Whatever the diagonal's author intended to leave behind, tonight it belongs to the interference layer more than to you.

turns.summary: Cellaceron returns to camp, reports the seam and the two-author mark, and Teva applies liverwort to resonance-burn and nosebleed — a quiet moment of care in the Zone dark.
turns.narration:
The nosebleed has slowed to a smear by the time you push up from the alcove floor, oilcloth square pressed to your upper lip. You take the beeswax taper from its ledge — its warm light bobs as you cup it — and step back into the open dark of the Zone. Sixty paces west. The berm-top is just visible as a darker edge against the shimmer-haze, and then Aevi's motes resolve out of the night, blue-teal and close-set, tracking your approach before you've cleared the last root-shadow.

Teva is exactly where you left her: cross-legged at the alcove entry, the Waystaff laid across her knees, lips still moving in the four-second count. She stops when she hears your step — doesn't look startled, just finishes the count before she looks up. You sit across from her and give it plain: the scored mark has two authors in two eras; the diagonal is older, cut by someone with a precise, confident hand who knew the corridor and chose to correct the survey cross rather than erase it; and beneath that groove a subsurface seam runs northeast-by-east, intersecting the corridor approximately forty paces in. The three temporal layers you reached for with Keen Appraisal gave you nothing clean — the Zone folded the read back on itself and charged interest in blood. Aevi drifts lower, motes contracting slightly, and one brightens: acknowledgment of the seam, not surprise.

Teva absorbs it without rushing. 'Northeast-by-east,' she says quietly, and her lips press together in the way that means she's counting — not the four-second interval, but something else, a bearing calculation you can see her running behind her eyes. 'That's not where the count pulls.' She doesn't say it like a contradiction. She says it like a second data point, and files it.

You ask her about the ointment — the liverwort mat in your pack, the burn along your forearms from the Greenlore surge, the dull ache behind your eyes from the failed Appraisal. And something beneath the practical: you've been alone in a dark alcove with a choked read and a nosebleed, and her hands would help. She doesn't make anything of the asking. She sets the Waystaff aside, takes the liverwort mat from you, folds it against her palm to bruise the cells — the green smell of it rises into the night air — and works it carefully along the inside of your forearms where the resonance-burn has left the skin tender and faintly warm. Her touch is deliberate and unhurried. At the nosebleed: she tips your chin with two fingers and presses the liverwort to the bridge until the last of it seals. 'You'll want to rest before you try to read that mark again,' she says, which is exactly what Farren would say, and probably where she learned it.

Aevi holds position at the berm-crest. It doesn't comment on any of this. Its motes are the warmest they've been since you crossed the shimmer.

turns.summary: Cellaceron reads Teva's emotional state during care — tiredness, old solitude, and a small question satisfied.
turns.narration:
You let her work. Teva's hands are competent — she presses the bruised liverwort mat against your forearm with the flat of her thumb, not hard, just steady — and while she does you watch her face. Prism Sight at a conserved hum, barely a flicker behind your eyes, the way you'd hold a candle near a page without setting it alight.
